visits.text: Long drive from Haridwar (about 7 hours). Arrive in Agra early afternoon if you leave early. Drop your things and rush to the Taj Mahal - preferably late afternoon when the golden light sets the white marble ablaze. Shah Jahan had it built between 1632 and 1653 in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died giving birth to their fourteenth child. 20,000 workers and 1,000 elephants labored for 22 years. The central dome rises to 73 meters, and the semi-precious stone inlays (carnelian, lapis lazuli, turquoise, jade) in white marble are mind-blowingly delicate. UNESCO-listed and voted one of the New Seven Wonders of the World in 2007.

2.5km upstream along the Yamuna, Agra Fort is the city's other jewel. Built in red sandstone by Emperor Akbar from 1565, then embellished in white marble by Shah Jahan, it served as the Mughal imperial residence for generations. This is where Shah Jahan spent his last eight years, imprisoned by his own son Aurangzeb, with only a view of the Taj Mahal he'd built for his wife. From the octagonal tower of Musamman Burj, the perspective on the Taj is heartbreaking.

Late afternoon, cross the river to Mehtab Bagh ('moonlight garden'), situated directly opposite the Taj Mahal across the Yamuna. It's the best vantage point to photograph the monument at sunset, without the main site's crowds. The garden has been restored and the manicured lawns offer a peaceful setting.

Day dedicated to Agra's surroundings. Start with Fatehpur Sikri (37km, 45 min), the 'city of victory' built by Emperor Akbar between 1571 and 1585 as the new Mughal capital. The site has been UNESCO-listed since 1986. Akbar had it built after visiting Sufi saint Sheikh Salim Chisti, who predicted an heir's birth - a prediction that came true. But the city was abandoned after just 14 years, probably due to water shortage. Result: a perfectly preserved red sandstone ghost city, without later centuries' additions.

At Fatehpur Sikri, don't miss the Panch Mahal, a five-story palace entirely open to the winds, built on the Persian windcatcher tower model. The Diwan-i-Khas (Hall of Private Audiences) is famous for its central pillar carved in Gujarati style, from which four walkways branch - Akbar received representatives of different religions here for his famous interfaith debates. Buland Darwaza (Victory Gate), 54 meters high, is one of the world's largest entrance gates.

Within the mosque enclosure, Sheikh Salim Chisti's tomb is a white marble jewel inlaid with jalis (pierced stone screens) of extraordinary delicacy. Childless women come to tie red threads making a wish - the tradition has lasted over four centuries.

Returning toward Agra, stop at Itimad-ud-Daulah's tomb, nicknamed the 'Baby Taj.' Built between 1622 and 1628 by Empress Nur Jahan for her father, it's the first Mughal monument entirely covered in white marble with semi-precious stone inlays - the pietra dura technique that would reach its peak at the Taj Mahal a few years later. The surrounding garden is far less crowded than the Taj and equally photogenic.

activities.text: If you're an early riser, return to the Taj Mahal at dawn - the gates open at sunrise and morning mist over the Yamuna creates an unreal atmosphere. Crowds are much smaller than during the day. This is when professional photographers choose to shoot.

Wander through Kinari Bazaar, Agra's old market near Jama Masjid. Artisans work marble inlaid with semi-precious stones (pietra dura) using the same techniques employed to decorate the Taj Mahal. You'll see workshops open to the street where artisans cut, polish, and inlay by hand.
Taste petha, Agra's signature sweet - a translucent candy made from caramelized white gourd, flavored with rose, saffron, or coconut. Shops around the Taj offer dozens of varieties.

Visit a pietra dura (marble marquetry) craft workshop in Agra's artisan quarter. Artisans will show you techniques inherited from Mughal times - cutting, polishing, and inlaying lapis lazuli, malachite, and carnelian in marble. Count 30 to 45 minutes for a complete demonstration.

If you have time, push on to Akbar's tomb at Sikandra (10km north of Agra). The emperor who advocated religious tolerance designed his own mausoleum - an astonishing mix of Hindu, Muslim, Buddhist, and Christian elements. The park houses herds of deer and langur monkeys.

visits.text: Fort Kochi's Chinese fishing nets (cheena vala) are Kerala's most iconic image. These cantilevered fishing nets, introduced by Chinese merchants in the 14th century, unfold at sunset against a blazing sky. In the morning, fishermen haul in their catch and you can buy fresh fish on the spot—nearby stalls will cook it for you in minutes.

The Jewish quarter (Jew Town) and the Paradesi Synagogue date from 1568—it's the oldest active synagogue in the Commonwealth. The floor is paved with hand-painted Chinese porcelain tiles, each one unique. Kochi's Jewish community, established here since the 10th century, now numbers only a few families. The surrounding streets overflow with antique shops and spice vendors.

St. Francis Church, built by the Portuguese in 1503, is the oldest European church in India. Vasco da Gama was buried here in 1524 before his remains were repatriated to Portugal. Next door, the Mattancherry Palace (known as the "Dutch Palace") houses remarkable 16th-century wall frescoes illustrating episodes from the Ramayana.

activities.text: Sunset behind the Chinese fishing nets is Kerala's most photographed moment. Settle on the Fort Kochi promenade around 5:30pm—the silhouettes of the nets stand out against the orange sky. Afterward, head up Princess Street to dine at one of the fish restaurants in the quarter.
Take the road to Alappuzha (55 km, 1.5 hours) in late afternoon. You'll follow the coast and the lagoons that herald the backwaters.

days_plan.itin_title: Tamil Nadu and Kerala Road Trip: Temples and Backwaters

visits.text: The descent from Munnar to Alleppey (165 km, 4.5 hours) is as spectacular as the ascent: you leave the tea estates, crossing spice forests, waterfalls, and Tamil villages before returning to the coastal plain. The lower you drop, the more heat and humidity return—brace yourself for the shift. Stop en route for lunch at a "meals" restaurant (local canteen): rice, sambar, rasam, and vegetables served on a banana leaf.

Arriving in Alleppey, board a kettuvallam—these traditional houseboats, 20 to 25 meters long, built from jackwood planks assembled without a single nail, bound with coir rope (coconut fiber), and waterproofed with fish oil. Once used to transport rice from the flooded paddies of Kuttanad, they've been converted into floating accommodations with bedroom, bathroom, and galley. You glide through a labyrinth of coconut-fringed canals, passing fishermen casting nets and children waving from the banks.

Wake on the water—breakfast served on the boat deck, facing the silent morning canals, is one of those moments that alone justify the journey. The morning cruise passes through Kuttanad, nicknamed the "rice bowl of Kerala": it's one of the few places on earth where rice is grown 1.5 to 2 meters below sea level. The FAO has designated this farming system a Globally Important Agricultural Heritage System.

After disembarking, explore the village on foot to observe daily life along the canals: coir rope making, fish drying, women in saris washing clothes on the ghats. Locals are welcoming and curious—a smile and a "namaskaram" open every door.

For deeper immersion, board a wooden canoe—these flat-bottomed craft slip into narrow channels where houseboats can't go. You'll glide beneath tunnels of tropical vegetation, threading between water lilies and kingfishers. Count on 1 to 1.5 hours for this silent excursion.

activities.text: Dine aboard the boat—the cook prepares Keralan dishes with ingredients bought that very morning from riverside villages. Karimeen pollichathu (pearl spot grilled in a banana leaf) is the local specialty not to miss. Watch the sun set from the upper deck, with Vembanad Lake—India's longest lake at 96 km—turning shades of orange and rose. Boats must moor before nightfall so as not to interfere with fishing.

Spend the afternoon birdwatching along the Vembanad banks. Herons, cormorants, egrets, kingfishers—the diversity is remarkable. If you're into photography, the late-afternoon light on the canals offers perfect conditions. Finish the day with a stroll on Alleppey Beach, a long ribbon of golden sand facing the Arabian Sea, often deserted by tourists who stick to the backwaters.

visits.text: You arrive in Amritsar by train (count around 6 hours from Delhi on the Shatabdi Express). Drop your bags and head straight for the Golden Temple - Harmandir Sahib. It's Sikhism's holiest site, and probably one of the world's most beautiful religious buildings. The temple sits at the center of a 150m-by-150m sacred pool (the Amrit Sarovar, 'pool of nectar'), connected to land by a 60-meter marble causeway. Maharaja Ranjit Singh covered the upper stories with 750kg of pure gold in the 1830s. Four entrances symbolize openness to all castes and religions. Arrive early in the morning to watch sunrise on the golden dome - the reflection in the water is breathtaking.

Right next to the Golden Temple, push through the narrow gate of Jallianwala Bagh. This is where on April 13, 1919, British General Dyer ordered his troops to fire on an unarmed crowd of thousands gathered for the Baisakhi festival. About 379 dead officially - likely far more. Bullet marks are still visible on the walls, and the well into which dozens jumped to escape the gunfire is preserved. The Flame of Liberty memorial, a 9-meter red sandstone pylon, was inaugurated in 1961. The place is stark and poignant - take time to read the panels.

Don't miss the Partition Museum, housed in Amritsar's old town hall. It traces the 1947 division of India and Pakistan through testimonies, personal objects, and photographs. It's one of the few museums worldwide dedicated to this event, which displaced 15 million people and killed between one and two million.

activities.text: Late afternoon, drive to the Wagah border (28km, 45 min). Every evening at sunset, Indian and Pakistani border guards perform the 'Beating Retreat' ceremony - a surreal spectacle of high kicks, exaggerated martial postures, and glares, followed by the simultaneous lowering of both flags. 25,000 spectators on the Indian side, a soccer stadium atmosphere. Arrive at least 90 minutes early to get a good seat.

Eat at the Golden Temple langar - the world's largest free kitchen. Every day over 50,000 meals are prepared and served by volunteers: dal, chapati, vegetables, and kheer. Everyone is welcome, without distinction. Sit in rows on the floor and let yourself be served. The experience is as spiritual as it is gastronomic.

If you return from Wagah in the evening, go back to the Golden Temple at night. The illuminated building reflects in the pool - it's even more magical than by day. Chants from the Guru Granth Sahib echo in the cool air and devotees circle the pool. A moment of absolute peace after the tumult of the Wagah ceremony.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: Welcome to Hampi - a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1986, this is India's largest field of ruins. In 1500, Vijayanagara was the world's second-largest city after Beijing, with 500,000 inhabitants and merchants from Persia and Portugal. More than 1,600 monuments spread across 42 km² amid a landscape of granite boulders and rice paddies. Allow at least 2.5 days to explore the site.

Start with Virupaksha Temple, the living heart of Hampi. Dedicated to Shiva, this 7th-century sanctuary is the only temple still in active worship on the site. The 50-meter gopuram dominates the entire bazaar. Inside, look for the ceiling paintings dating from the 14th and 16th centuries - they depict scenes from the Ramayana and Mahabharata. King Krishnadevaraya added the great pillared hall in 1510 to mark his coronation. Arrive early in the morning: the light is beautiful and the monkeys are still calm.

Next, climb Hemakuta Hill, dotted with small Jain and Hindu temples from the 14th-15th centuries, including Ganagitti (consecrated in 1386). The real draw is the view: from the summit, you overlook the entire site on one side and the Tungabhadra on the other. Late in the day, the granite boulders take on an almost surreal golden hue. Settle on the rocks with water - this is the ultimate sunset spot.

Descend through Hampi Bazaar, the ancient market street in front of Virupaksha. In the 16th century, Portuguese travelers described stalls overflowing with precious stones, spices and Arabian horses. Today, the granite columns that lined the shops still stand - imagine the bustle of this artery at its peak.

Head to Vijaya Vittala Temple, the absolute jewel of Hampi. Begun under Devaraya II (1422-1446) and enriched by Krishnadevaraya (1509-1529), it occupies an enclosure measuring 164 m by 94 m. The star attraction is the Stone Chariot - a stone chariot dedicated to Garuda, inspired by Konark's Sun Temple after Krishnadevaraya discovered it during a campaign in Odisha. The wheels were once mobile, now cemented in place. The complex is open from 8:30am to 5:30pm.

In the Ranga Mantapa, you'll discover the 56 musical pillars nicknamed 'SaReGaMa'. By gently tapping each pillar, you could obtain sounds of mridangam, cymbals or veena. The British, intrigued, sawed two of them open to unlock the secret - finding nothing inside. The two cut pillars are still visible. Today, touching the pillars is forbidden, but a guard sometimes gives a demonstration.

Continue to the Elephant Stables: 11 aligned domes - some round, others with Islamic cupolas - which housed the royal elephants. It's one of the most striking examples of the empire's Indo-Islamic style. Right next door, the Zenana Enclosure houses the Lotus Mahal, a two-story pavilion whose arches recall an opening lotus flower - a cool retreat designed for the queens.

Make a detour to the Lakshmi Narasimha monolith (6.7 m high), Vishnu in his man-lion form. Push on to Krishna Temple and the Sasivekalu Ganesha - a 2.4 m monolith carved from a single block, named 'mustard seed' for the shape of his belly.

Rise early to climb Anjanadri Hill - the presumed birthplace of the god Hanuman according to the Ramayana. Count on 575 steps (30-40 minutes) between granite blocks. At the summit, a small temple houses a Hanuman idol carved from rock. The real reward is the 360° view: checkered rice paddies, coconut groves, the serpentine Tungabhadra, and all of Hampi spread across the horizon. Go at sunrise. Watch out for monkeys: don't show any food.

Once down, push on to Pampa Sarovar, a sacred basin covered with lotuses - one of Hinduism's five holy lakes. According to legend, this is where the goddess Pampa did penance to marry Shiva. Two small temples border the basin. The place is peaceful and rarely crowded.

Visit Kodandarama Temple on the banks of the Tungabhadra, where Rama is said to have crowned Sugriva king of the monkeys. The bas-reliefs tell the scene with finesse. The setting is magnificent: the river flows just below, lined with water-polished granite blocks.

activities.text: Cross the Tungabhadra in a coracle - these round bamboo boats covered in tar have been used here for centuries. The journey takes 5-10 minutes and drops you on the Anegundi side. The service runs from roughly 7am to 6pm, but depends on water levels (avoid monsoon season). On the Anegundi side, you'll find homestays with views over the rice fields - far more authentic than Hospet.

Set yourself up on the Hemakuta rocks or the north bank for sunset. The granite blocks naturally stacked over billions of years take on orange tones as the sun sinks behind the hills. If you're staying on the Anegundi side, the peace is total - no roads, just the sound of the river.

In the afternoon, rent a bicycle or scooter on the Anegundi side and explore the north bank. You'll pass Gagan Mahal palace (16th-century royal residence with Indo-Islamic balconies), Ranganatha Temple and the fort ruins. Artisans still make objects from banana fiber - a unique local tradition.

Late in the day, descend to Sanapur Lake (4 km from Anegundi): turquoise water between granite blocks, with the possibility of a coracle tour. In the evening, Anegundi is very quiet - perfect for digesting the kilometers of ruins.

Explore the prehistoric rock paintings of Onake Kindi, a few kilometers from Anegundi. These Neolithic paintings testify to human occupation well before the Vijayanagara empire. By scooter, the road through rice paddies and granite chaos is a pleasure.

Organizing departure to Goa: from Hospet Junction (13 km), the Howrah Express departs 4 days/week to Madgaon (7-8 hours). Private overnight buses reach Margao in 8-10 hours. From Margao, count on 40 minutes by bus or taxi to Palolem. Book in advance - berths go fast.

visits.text: The road from Pune to Aurangabad (235 km, 4.5 hours) crosses the Deccan plateau—a landscape of cotton fields, sugarcane, and dusty villages. Aurangabad is your base for the next three days and Maharashtra's two UNESCO jewels: Ajanta and Ellora.

Upon arrival, visit Bibi Ka Maqbara, the mausoleum built in 1660 by Azam Shah, son of Mughal emperor Aurangzeb, in memory of his mother Dilras Banu. It's called the "Poor Man's Taj" or "Taj of the Deccan"—and with good reason: it's an unabashed replica of the Taj Mahal, but executed on a much more modest budget. The base is marble, but the upper portion is stucco painted to imitate marble. The result is both touching and fascinating: same proportions, same Mughal gardens, same symmetry, but a finish that betrays the financial constraints. Far from being a "fake Taj," it's a monument that tells a story of filial love and thwarted ambition.

Early afternoon, push on to Daulatabad Fort, 15 km from Aurangabad—a 12th-century fortress perched on a 200-meter-high conical hill, considered one of India's most impregnable forts. The craziest story: in 1327, Sultan Muhammad bin Tughlaq decided to move the entire capital from Delhi here—forcing hundreds of thousands of people to march over 1,000 km across the Deccan. The plan failed miserably and he eventually sent everyone back to Delhi. The climb to the summit passes through dark passages, crocodile moats (now dry), and a spiral tunnel designed to trap invaders in the dark. The view from the top embraces the entire Deccan plateau.

Full day dedicated to Ajanta Caves—plan an early morning departure from Aurangabad (100 km, 2.5 hours drive). Thirty Buddhist caves carved into the horseshoe-shaped cliff above the Waghora River, UNESCO-listed since 1983. They were excavated in two phases: the first in the 2nd century BC by monks of the Hinayana tradition, the second in the 5th-6th centuries under the Vakatakas, a period of artistic genius without parallel. The caves were abandoned, forgotten, engulfed by jungle for over 1,000 years—until a British officer on a tiger hunt, John Smith, rediscovered them in 1819.

Cave 1 is the absolute masterpiece. The murals—technically tempera on dry plaster, not frescoes—are among the oldest surviving Indian paintings. The Bodhisattva Padmapani, painted on either side of the sanctuary entrance, is Ajanta's most famous image: a being of troubling beauty, eyes half-closed in compassion, adorned with jewels but his gaze turned inward. The duality between sensuality and spirituality is strikingly modern. Opposite, the Vajrapani, protector wielding the thunderbolt, completes the duo.

Don't miss Cave 17, which preserves the site's most colorful and best-preserved paintings—scenes from Buddha's life with a chromatic vivacity that defies the centuries. Cave 26 houses a 7-meter reclining Buddha, in parinirvana (entering final nirvana), carved from rock with a serenity that stops you dead. Cave 19, a richly decorated chaitya, shows the Buddha in anthropomorphic form—a turning point in Buddhist art.

A mandatory shuttle connects the parking lot to the caves (4 km). The entire site requires 4 to 5 hours to visit. Bring water, a flashlight (the caves are dark), comfortable shoes, and patience—crowds can be heavy in high season. Flash photography is prohibited to protect the paintings. REMINDER: CLOSED MONDAYS.

Full day at Ellora Caves, just 30 km from Aurangabad. This UNESCO site of 34 caves is unique in the world: Buddhist (caves 1-12), Hindu (caves 13-29), and Jain (caves 30-34) temples carved side by side into the same basalt cliff, between the 6th and 11th centuries. This architectural coexistence of three different religions is an exceptional testimony to religious tolerance in ancient India.

The Kailasa Temple (Cave 16) is THE monument that alone justifies the journey. Imagine: an entire temple carved from top to bottom from a single basalt block, starting from the roof and working down—the reverse of all classical construction. The 8th-century architects, under King Krishna I of the Rashtrakuta dynasty, had between 150,000 and 200,000 tons of rock extracted to sculpt this temple dedicated to Shiva. The result: a structure 50 meters long, 33 meters wide, and 30 meters high—twice the surface area of Athens' Parthenon. The sculptures adorning the walls—Ravana lifting Mount Kailash, Shiva and Parvati, life-size elephants—are dramatically powerful. An ancient text from 812 AD reports that the architect himself, upon seeing his completed work, exclaimed: "How did I build this?"

Next, explore the Buddhist caves—Cave 10 (Vishvakarma or "Carpenter's Cave") is a spectacular chaitya with a vault imitating wooden framework. Cave 12 (Teen Tal) is a three-story monastery carved from rock, of impressive monumentality. The Jain caves (30-34), later in date, are extraordinarily finely chiseled—the details of the sculptures and painted ceilings are almost jeweler-like in precision.

activities.text: If time allows, detour to Aurangabad Caves—12 Buddhist caves from the 6th-7th centuries, far less known than Ajanta and Ellora but remarkable for their female sculptures of exceptional grace. You'll likely have them all to yourself.

The late afternoon return to Aurangabad is the moment to breathe. The road passes through rural Maharashtra landscapes—sorghum fields, buffalo herds, village temples. Dine at one of Aurangabad's restaurants—Marathwada cuisine is robust and spicy, with specialties like naan qalia (spiced lamb) inherited from Mughal cuisine.

Before leaving Ellora, detour to Grishneshwar Temple, 2 km from the caves—one of the 12 Jyotirlinga, Shiva's most sacred temples in India. The current temple dates from the 18th century, but the site has been sacred for much longer. The atmosphere of devotion is intense. REMINDER: Ellora is CLOSED TUESDAYS.

visits.text: Badami boasts four rock-cut temples carved into red sandstone cliffs between the 6th and 8th centuries by the Chalukya dynasty. Cave Temple 1, dedicated to Shiva, is the oldest—you'll see a sculpture of Nataraja (dancing Shiva) displaying 81 codified dance positions. Cave Temple 3, the largest and most impressive, is dedicated to Vishnu and dates to 578 CE—the date is carved in stone. The sculptures are remarkably refined for their era. Wear good shoes—the stairways cut into the rock are steep and slippery.

Below the caves, Agastya Lake forms a turquoise mirror framed by sandstone cliffs. Walk around it (about 30 minutes) to discover small temples and shrines carved into the rock on the opposite shore. If you're feeling brave, climb to Badami Fort via the trail starting from the lake's north side—the view from the fort ruins embraces the entire valley and the caves below.

activities.text: Make the most of your day in Badami by taking an excursion to Pattadakal and Aihole, respectively 22 and 34 km away. This Chalukya archaeological triangle is UNESCO-listed (for Pattadakal). At Pattadakal, you'll see about ten temples from the 7th-8th centuries blending Dravidian (southern) and Nagara (northern) styles. Aihole, often called the cradle of Hindu temple architecture, counts more than 120 temples scattered across a plateau. Allow half a day for both sites together.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: If you're arriving from Puducherry by overnight bus or car (around 5-6 hours via NH77/NH44), you'll arrive at Majestic bus terminal right in the heart of Bengaluru. Take advantage of this stop to arrange your bus or train tickets to Hampi - Kempegowda bus station is Karnataka's main hub, with daily KSRTC buses departing for Hospet (7-8 hours by road). Book the day before if you're traveling during peak season (October-February).

Cubbon Park is Bengaluru's green lung: 120 hectares of gardens created in 1870 by General Sankey, with over 6,000 trees - from gulmohar with brilliant red flowers to imposing silver oaks. You'll also find the State Central Library, a superb red-painted Gothic building, and the Government Museum, one of India's oldest (founded in 1876). If you're there on a Sunday, the park is closed to cars and transforms into a venue for yoga, concerts and a small farmers' market.

Right next to the park, don't miss Vidhana Soudha: this massive neo-Dravidian building, constructed in 1954, houses Karnataka's parliament. You can't go inside, but the illuminated granite facade in the evening is worth a detour. Opposite, Attara Kacheri (1864) with its Corinthian columns houses the High Court - a striking contrast between colonial architecture and Indian style.

activities.text: Stroll down MG Road, Bengaluru's historic shopping artery. It's less about the shopping than the atmosphere - trendy cafes, bookshops and old-school stores coexist in a distinctly Bangalorean energy. If you're hungry, head to VV Puram Food Street (3 km south) to sample crispy dosas and fluffy bondas.

Preparing for departure to Hampi: from Majestic, overnight KSRTC buses depart between 8pm and 10pm for Hospet (arriving around 6-7am). If you prefer the train, the Hampi Express (#16592) leaves Bengaluru at 10pm and arrives at Hospet Junction around 7:10am. From there, it's 13 km by auto-rickshaw to the ruins. Take a sleeper bus or train berth to sleep en route and save a day.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Hampi, Goa and the Nilgiri Hills

visits.text: Train from Kolkata to Bhubaneswar (about 7 hours on the Shatabdi). The 'city of a thousand temples' closes this circuit with a return to Indian spirituality's roots. Lingaraj Temple, Bhubaneswar's largest and most venerated, dates from the 11th century. Its 55-meter tower (deul) dominates the city - it's the finest example of Kalinga architectural style. Entry is reserved for Hindus, but you can observe the temple from a raised platform outside the enclosure, originally installed for Lord Curzon.

Rajarani Temple (11th century) is nicknamed the 'love temple' for its sculptures of entwined couples and celestial nymphs recalling Khajuraho, on a smaller scale. Unlike Lingaraj, it's open to all. The surrounding garden is peaceful - the golden sandstone sculptures in late afternoon light are magnificent.

Visit Udayagiri and Khandagiri caves, carved in the 2nd century BC by King Kharavela to house Jain monks. Udayagiri ('sunrise hill') has 18 caves adorned with detailed bas-reliefs showing court scenes and elephant processions. Rani Gumpha ('queen's cave'), on two levels, is the most spectacular. Khandagiri, opposite, offers a panoramic view over Bhubaneswar from its summit.

If you have time, detour to Dhauli (8km south), where Emperor Ashoka, after the bloody Battle of Kalinga in 261 BC that killed over 100,000, had a crisis of conscience that converted him to Buddhism. The rock edicts of Ashoka carved in stone are among India's oldest texts. The white Shanti Stupa (Peace Stupa), built in 1972 by the Japanese at the hilltop, offers clear views over rice paddies.

activities.text: Explore Ekamra Haat market, a permanent craft market where Odisha artisans display their specialties: stone carvings, patachitra (cloth paintings), Cuttack silver filigree jewelry, and ikat fabrics. It's the ideal spot for quality souvenirs at reasonable prices.

Taste Odisha cuisine: dalma (lentils with vegetables and coconut), pakhala bhata (rice fermented in water - Odisha's emblematic dish, served cold), and chhena poda (caramelized fresh cheese cake), a specialty unavailable elsewhere in India.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: The Gol Gumbaz in Bijapur (now Vijayapura) houses the world's second-largest unsupported dome after St. Peter's in Rome, with an interior diameter of 37.9 meters. Built in 1656 as the mausoleum of Sultan Mohammed Adil Shah, its whispering gallery at the dome's base amplifies the slightest sound spectacularly—a whisper from one side is clearly heard at the other end, 37 meters away. Climb the spiral staircase (seven floors) to access this gallery and enjoy a panoramic view over the city. Mind your ears—the noise can be deafening when school groups shout inside.

One kilometer west, don't miss Ibrahim Rauza, often compared to a miniature Taj Mahal. This mausoleum and its adjoining mosque were built between 1615 and 1627 for Ibrahim Adil Shah II. The proportions are remarkably elegant, with finely carved arches, delicate minarets, and calligraphic stone inscriptions. Legend has it that Shah Jahan drew inspiration from it when designing the Taj Mahal. The site is much less crowded than Gol Gumbaz—take your time to savor it.

activities.text: Bijapur deserves some aimless wandering through its lanes: the citadel ramparts (6.5 km perimeter), Jami Masjid mosque with its 2,250 prayer spaces, and the numerous tombs and pavilions of the Adil Shahi sultans are scattered throughout the city. Rent an auto-rickshaw by the hour to cover the more distant sites. Late in the day, sunset from the ramparts is a welcome moment of calm after a day of sightseeing.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: Road from Varanasi (250km, about 5 hours). Bodhgaya is Buddhism's holiest site - this is where Siddhartha Gautama attained Enlightenment under a fig tree (pipal) about 2,500 years ago, becoming the Buddha. The Mahabodhi Temple, UNESCO-listed since 2002, marks this exact spot. The 55-meter pyramidal tower, rebuilt between the 5th and 6th centuries, dominates the site. Behind the temple, the descendant of the original Bodhi tree still stands - you'll see monks from all Buddhist traditions meditating at its feet, from Tibetans in maroon robes to Thais in orange.

Tour the international monasteries surrounding Mahabodhi Temple - each Buddhist country has built its own in its architectural style. The Thai monastery with its golden roof, the minimalist Japanese temple, the colorful Tibetan monastery, the Chinese temple, and the Burmese temple offer a Buddhist world tour in a single walk. The whole area covers about 2km and is easily done on foot.

The Great Buddha Statue (25 meters tall), inaugurated in 1989 by the Dalai Lama, sits in a Japanese garden south of the main temple. It depicts Buddha in meditation posture, seated on a lotus - the 10 smaller statues at the base represent his principal disciples.

activities.text: Join a guided meditation session at one of the monasteries - several offer free sessions for visitors, usually in the morning. The experience of meditating at the very place where Buddha attained Enlightenment has particular resonance, whether you're Buddhist or not.

In the evening, return to Mahabodhi Temple for evening prayers. Monks light hundreds of lamps around the temple and Bodhi tree - the atmosphere is deeply calm. The contrast with Varanasi's frenetic energy is total.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: Raniji ki Baori is the largest of Bundi's 50 stepwells—and one of Rajasthan's most impressive. Built in 1699 by Queen Nathavati Ji Solanki, it plunges 46 meters deep. You enter on the second level through an arched portal flanked by marble elephant statues. Each level houses a place of worship, and the pillars are carved with figures of Vishnu—look for his incarnations as Varaha (boar), Matsya (fish) and Narasimha (man-lion). The entrance is small and easy to miss on the main street.

The Garh Palace seems to emerge from the cliff face—seen from below, the effect is striking. Built by Rao Raja Ratan Singh between 1607 and 1631, it's renowned for its turquoise and gold frescoes of the Bundi miniature painting school, created with natural pigments (minerals, vegetables, precious stones, silver and gold). Enter through the Hathi Pol (Elephant Gate), climb to the Diwan-e-Aam, then push on to the Chhatra Mahal and Badal Mahal. Warning: many rooms are closed and left to the bats—a flashlight is useful.

Taragarh Fort (Star Fort) crowns the hill above Garh Palace. Built in the 14th century, it offers a panoramic view over Bundi, its blue houses and the Aravalli. The climb is steep—bring good shoes and water. At the top: labyrinthine passages, crumbling bastions and rock-cut reservoirs. No infrastructure, no crowds—raw Rajasthan.

Bundi resembles a miniature Jodhpur, minus the tourists. Blue houses, narrow lanes and vintage shops evoke another century. Wander through the fruit and vegetable market in the town center, where merchants wear imposing colorful turbans. This is also where Rudyard Kipling reportedly found inspiration for Kim—he stayed at Sukh Mahal, a small palace on the edge of Jait Sagar lake.

activities.text: Set out to discover Bundi's hidden baoris—the town once had about a hundred. Dhabhai Kund, not far from Raniji ki Baori, is a massive 17th-century symmetrical well—possibly India's second largest. Also seek out Nagar Sagar Kund, twin wells near Chougan Gate, often overlooked amid the bustle of the shops.

days_plan.itin_title: Intensive Rajasthan Road Trip: Forts, Tigers & Taj Mahal

visits.text: Chandigarh is a transit stop between Manali and Rishikesh (310km from Manali, about 8 hours drive). But the city is worth a pause: it's the only Indian city planned by Le Corbusier in the 1950s, after Partition. The Capitol Complex, UNESCO-listed in 2016, groups the High Court, Secretariat, and Assembly - a monumental raw concrete ensemble that startles in the Indian context. The Open Hand, a 26-meter-tall sculpture, turns freely with the wind and symbolizes peace.

Don't miss the Rock Garden, the work of one man - Nek Chand, a road inspector who secretly built a sculpture garden from recycled urban waste (pottery shards, construction debris, broken bangles) on 16 hectares of government land. Discovered in 1975, the site was threatened with demolition, but popular support saved it. Today it's India's second most visited site after the Taj Mahal. The mosaic figurines lined up by the hundreds create a striking visual effect.

If you have an hour free, walk along Sukhna Lake, an artificial lake created in 1958 by building a dam across a seasonal stream. Locals take their evening stroll here, and boats are available. The late-day light on the water with the Shivaliks in the background is superb.

activities.text: Take a tour of Sector 17, Chandigarh's pedestrian shopping center, designed by Le Corbusier. The arcades and plazas recall a European city transplanted to India. It's a good place to eat - Punjabi cuisine here is among the country's best.

Visit the Rose Garden (Zakir Hussain Rose Garden), which spreads over 12 hectares and houses more than 1,600 rose varieties. It's Asia's largest rose garden - and in season (February-March), the spectacle is enchanting.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: The Kapaleeshwarar Temple in Mylapore is Chennai's most important Dravidian temple — dedicated to Shiva, it dates from the 7th century (rebuilt in the 16th). Its 37-metre gopuram covered in brightly painted deities towers over the city's oldest neighbourhood. Come in the morning — pilgrims in white dhotis circle the temple clockwise, and flower sellers occupy every nook.

Fort St George, built by the British East India Company in 1644, is the first English fort in India. It now serves as the seat of the Tamil Nadu government. The museum inside displays weapons, uniforms and letters from the colonial era — including correspondence from Robert Clive. St. Mary's Church (1680) within the compound is the oldest Anglican church east of Suez.

Marina Beach stretches for 13 km — it's the second-longest urban beach in the world. Swimming is inadvisable (strong currents), but the late-afternoon promenade is a spectacle: sundal vendors (spiced chickpeas), kites, families out for a stroll, cricket games. At the southern end, the neo-Gothic San Thome Cathedral (1896) is said to be built over the tomb of the Apostle Thomas.

activities.text: Explore Mylapore on foot — Chennai's oldest and liveliest neighbourhood. Around the Kapaleeshwarar Temple, the lanes are packed with Kanchipuram silk sari shops, jewellery stores, and small restaurants serving the best filter coffee in the city.

Prepare the Chennai → Varanasi leg. WARNING: this is the longest journey of the trip — roughly 22 hours by train (1,600 km). A domestic flight (2.5 hours, several carriers) is strongly recommended to avoid losing two travel days. If you're set on the train, the Chennai–Varanasi Express exists, but it's an endurance test. Book the flight well in advance.

days_plan.itin_title: India Grand Tour: From the Taj Mahal to the Backwaters
South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: Chittorgarh Fort is India's largest fort: 280 hectares enclosed by 4.5 km of ramparts, perched 180 meters above the plains. A UNESCO site since 2013, it has seven successive gates—Padan Pol, Bhairon Pol, Hanuman Pol, Ganesh Pol, Jorla Pol, Laxman Pol and Ram Pol—which you'll pass through driving up. The site contains 65 structures including 4 palaces, 19 temples and 2 towers. Around 3,000 people still live within the walls.

Rana Kumbha Palace, the most massive of the fort's ruins, dates in its earliest foundations to 734 under Bappa Rawal. Rebuilt by Maharana Kumbha in the 15th century, this is where Maharana Udai Singh was born—saved as a baby by his nursemaid Panna Dai, who reportedly hid him in a fruit basket to protect him from a usurper. The remains still show stables, water reservoirs and women's quarters (zenana). It's also the site of the terrible jauhars—the collective self-immolations of Rajput women facing invading armies.

You'll spot the Vijay Stambha (Tower of Victory) from afar—37 meters of red sandstone and white marble, 9 stories, 157 steps. Built between 1442 and 1449 by Rana Kumbha to celebrate his victory over the Sultan of Malwa. The interior is carved floor to ceiling: Hindu deities, seasonal allegories, weapons, musical instruments. On the fifth floor, look for portraits of architect Jaita and his three sons—a rare signature for the era. The view from the top embraces all of Chittorgarh.

Smaller at 22 meters but older, the Kirti Stambha (Tower of Fame) dates from the 12th century. Dedicated to Adinath, the first Jain master, it's adorned with Jain sculptures across its entire surface. The 54 steps lead to the top via a very narrow staircase—claustrophobics beware. It testifies to the religious coexistence that prevailed in the fort.

Make a detour to the Mirabai Temple, built in classic north Indian style. It commemorates the famous 16th-century mystic poet, a Krishna devotee whose fervor scandalized the royal court. The small sanctuary surrounded by an open porch with four pavilions radiates a soothing atmosphere after the intensity of the warrior ruins.

activities.text: Take a tuk-tuk or private vehicle to complete the full circuit of the fort—at 280 hectares, covering it on foot in a day is impossible. Allow 3 to 4 hours to link the palaces, towers and temples, photo stops included.
If you stay into evening, the sound and light show recounts the battles and jauhars of the fort under the stars. Timings vary by season—check on-site.

days_plan.itin_title: Intensive Rajasthan Road Trip: Forts, Tigers & Taj Mahal

visits.text: Train from Ernakulam Junction to Coimbatore Junction - about 3.5 hours, with departures roughly every 4 hours. The journey runs along the Western Ghats with beautiful views of tropical vegetation. On arrival, Coimbatore is an industrial city with little tourist charm, but it's the gateway to the Nilgiris and Isha Yoga Center.

Head to Isha Yoga Center, 30 km west of Coimbatore, at the foot of the Velliangiri mountains. The Adiyogi statue greets you: 34 meters high (112 feet), 45 meters long - it's the world's largest sculpted bust according to Guinness. Inaugurated in 2017, its height symbolizes the 112 pathways to liberation in yogic tradition. Entry is free.

Inside the complex, the Dhyanalinga is a unique meditation space - a dome of 250,000 bricks built without steel or cement, housing a consecrated linga. Whatever your beliefs, sit for a few minutes in the dome's silence. Sound offerings (chanting and Tibetan bowls) take place at 11:50am and 5:50pm. The Linga Bhairavi Temple, dedicated to the divine feminine aspect, is right next door.

activities.text: Every evening at 7pm, the Adiyogi Divya Darshanam sound and light show projects a 14-minute light fresco onto the giant statue, telling the story of yoga - it's visually impressive. Note: mobile phones are not allowed in the complex (lockers at the entrance).

If you want to go deeper, the center offers guided meditation sessions and baths in the Theerthakunds - Surya Kund (men) and Chandra Kund (women), consecrated water pools. The complex also has accommodation (cottages, dormitories) if you want to spend the night - but book ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Hampi, Goa and the Nilgiri Hills

visits.text: Day in the Nilgiris: climb to Coonoor (70 km, 2 hours of winding road from Coimbatore) - the second-largest hill station in the Nilgiris after Ooty, perched at 1,800 m altitude. The road climbs through eucalyptus and shola forests (high-altitude tropical forests) with hairpin bends. If you can, take the Nilgiri Mountain Railway (UNESCO heritage) from Mettupalayam - the rack railway climbs through tea plantations. Book ahead.

Dolphin's Nose is a natural viewpoint perched at 1,550 meters - a rocky spur resembling a dolphin's nose. In clear weather, the view embraces the valleys, tea plantations as far as the eye can see and, in the distance, Catherine Falls. The access road passes through kilometers of tea gardens - stop to photograph the pickers in colorful saris among the rows of tea bushes.

Highfield Tea Factory offers a guided tour of the tea-making process, from picking to drying. You'll see the rolling machines, oxidation vats and dryers. At the end, a tasting lets you compare white, green and black teas from the Nilgiris. The shop also sells eucalyptus oil and artisan chocolate. Open 9am to 7pm.

Today you're sleeping at altitude. From Coonoor (where you were yesterday for Dolphin's Nose and Highfield Tea Factory), take the Nilgiri Mountain Railway between Coonoor and Ooty - 19 km of UNESCO-listed rack railway, pulled by diesel locomotive through tea plantations and shola forests. The train passes through Wellington, Lovedale and Ketti before arriving in Ooty (Udhagamandalam) at 2,200 m altitude. The full Mettupalayam-Ooty route (46 km) takes 5 hours and crosses 16 tunnels and 250 bridges - it's India's only rack railway, built by the British and opened in 1899. Book your tickets in advance, especially in season (October-June).

In Ooty, visit the Government Botanical Gardens - 22 hectares of gardens founded in 1848 by the British, with a collection of over 1,000 plant species including fossilized trees 20 million years old. The terraced garden descends along a hillside, with a rose section, fern house and massive tropical trees. Allow 1-1.5 hours to visit. Entry is modest.

Descend late in the day toward Coonoor (20 minutes by train or 30 minutes by road). On the way or once there, stop at Sim's Park - a 12-hectare botanical garden nestled in a natural ravine, with species of pines, magnolias and trees endemic to the Nilgiris. The park is less crowded than Ooty's gardens and offers a very pleasant walk in a hilly setting.

activities.text: Multiple photo stops along the Nilgiris road - Lamb's Rock offers a plunging view over the Coimbatore plains and forests below. Monkeys are numerous and bold: keep your belongings in hand. Sim's Park (12 hectares of botanical garden) is also worth a visit if you have time.

Late in the day, instead of descending to Coimbatore, stay at altitude in Coonoor - accommodation is plentiful, from colonial homestays to resorts with valley views. The nighttime coolness (15°C) is a luxury after days in the southern heat. Tomorrow, you'll explore Ooty by rack railway before descending.

In the morning, trek to Droog Fort - an old military outpost of Tipu Sultan (18th century) perched at 1,800 m altitude, 15 km from Coonoor. Only a wall and watchtower remain, but the path crossing Nonsuch Estate's tea plantations (3-4 km walk) is superb. From the summit, the view plunges over the entire Coonoor valley. Watch for wild bison in the area - keep your distance. Bring good shoes and water, there's nothing at the top.

Evening in Coonoor: the cool altitude (15-20°C at night) is a welcome relief after the plains' heat. Enjoy a masala chai in one of the small shops in Coonoor bazaar, or try the artisan chocolates and local fruit jams - the region is known for its high-altitude products. Bring a sweater for the evening.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Hampi, Goa and the Nilgiri Hills
South India Circuit: Hampi, Goa and the Nilgiri Hills

visits.text: Start with the Red Fort, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2007 — its red sandstone walls rise 23 metres high and encircle a 100-hectare complex. Shah Jahan had it built between 1638 and 1648 as the imperial palace of his new capital, Shahjahanabad. Don't miss the Diwan-i-Khas, the Hall of Private Audiences, whose ceiling was once studded with precious stones. Every August 15th, India's Prime Minister hoists the national flag here — you'll spot the podium from the ramparts. Allow at least 1.5 hours, and arrive at opening time (9:30 AM) to beat the crowds.

Jama Masjid is a five-minute walk from the Red Fort — it's the largest mosque in India, built by Shah Jahan between 1644 and 1656. Its inner courtyard can hold 25,000 worshippers. Climb to the top of the south minaret (around 40 metres) for a 360° panorama over Old Delhi — you'll see the tangled rooftops of Chandni Chowk and, on a clear day, the Parliament dome in the distance. Remove your shoes at the entrance, and if you're wearing shorts, you'll be lent a sarong.

Dive into Chandni Chowk, Delhi's oldest commercial artery — Shah Jahan laid it out in 1650. It's a labyrinth of alleyways where spices, fabrics, jewellery and electronics jostle for space. Make your way to Paranthe Wali Gali, a narrow lane famous for its paranthas fried in ghee — stalls have been serving them here since the 1870s. Fair warning: the crowds are intense, so keep your belongings close.

Make a detour to Raj Ghat, Gandhi's memorial — a slab of black marble marks the spot where he was cremated on January 31, 1948, one day after his assassination. The site is simple and deeply moving, surrounded by lawns. An eternal flame burns here at all times. Allow 30 minutes, barefoot on the marble.

Head to New Delhi this morning — start with Humayun's Tomb, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1993. Built in 1570, it's the first great Mughal garden-tomb and the direct blueprint for the Taj Mahal, built 60 years later. The white marble dome stands 42 metres tall, surrounded by symmetrical gardens divided into squares (the Persian charbagh layout). Arrive early in the morning — the golden light on the red sandstone is stunning, and you'll have the place almost to yourself before 10 AM.

You can't miss the Qutub Minar — this 72.5-metre brick tower is the tallest minaret in the world made from this material. Construction began in 1192 under Qutb ud-Din Aibak, Delhi's first sultan, and was completed by his successor Iltutmish. Look closely at the Arabic inscriptions that cover the tower from base to top — each floor features a different balcony style. You can no longer climb to the top (closed after an accident in 1981), but the base is impressive. Right next to it, stop by the 4th-century Iron Pillar that has resisted rust for 1,600 years — a metallurgical mystery no one has cracked yet.

India Gate, Delhi's triumphal arch, stands 42 metres tall. It honours the 70,000 Indian soldiers who died during World War I. In the evening, the monument is lit up and the esplanade becomes a promenade for local families. It's a good spot for a midday break.

Finish at Lodi Gardens, a 36-hectare park that houses 15th-century tombs from the Sayyid and Lodi dynasties — octagonal buildings set amid immaculate lawns. Delhiites come here to jog or picnic. If you have time, seek out the Tomb of Mohammed Shah (1450) and the Bara Gumbad Mosque.

Back in Delhi — full circle. If you arrive on the morning Vande Bharat Express, you have the afternoon free. Visit what you missed on Days 1–2: the Akshardham Temple (2005) is a spectacular modern complex covering 40 hectares on the banks of the Yamuna — 20,000 statues carved in pink sandstone and white marble. Admission is free but security checks are strict (no phones or cameras allowed inside).

Hauz Khas Village, in south Delhi, centres on a 14th-century reservoir (built by Alauddin Khalji) surrounded by medieval ruins — now reimagined as a trendy quarter with art galleries, designer boutiques and rooftop restaurants. The ruins of the madrasa and the tomb of Firoz Shah Tughlaq overlook the lake — a peaceful and photogenic spot.

activities.text: Take a cycle-rickshaw ride through the alleyways of Old Delhi — it's the best way to weave between market stalls and sacred cows. Negotiate the fare before hopping on (expect around 30–45 minutes for a full tour of the neighbourhood). You'll pass the Gurudwara Sis Ganj Sahib, a 17th-century Sikh temple you can enter freely — and even enjoy a free meal at the langar, the communal kitchen.

In the evening, if you still have energy, catch the Red Fort's sound and light show — the 45-minute spectacle traces Mughal history with the illuminated walls as a backdrop. The English show starts around 8:30 PM (check times on-site, as they change with the season).

In the late afternoon, walk to the Dargah of Hazrat Nizamuddin — Delhi's most revered Sufi shrine, founded in the 14th century. Every Thursday evening, musicians perform qawwali in the courtyard — the atmosphere is electric, somewhere between devotion and trance. Arrive around 6 PM to settle in before the crowd thickens.

Get ready for your departure to Agra tomorrow morning: the Gatiman Express leaves Hazrat Nizamuddin station at 8:10 AM and arrives at Agra Cantonment at 9:50 AM — that's 100 minutes for 188 km, making it one of India's fastest trains. Book your tickets on IRCTC at least 3–4 days ahead. AC Chair Car is recommended. Note: this train does not run on Fridays.

Last-minute shopping at Khan Market or Connaught Place — for souvenirs, spices, tea, textiles. Khan Market is Delhi's most upscale shopping area, with excellent bookshops and cafés. Connaught Place (CP) is a circle of white colonnades built in 1933 — more down-to-earth, livelier, with the metro right below.

Farewell dinner — Delhi is the gastronomic capital of North India. Butter chicken (invented here in the 1950s), chole bhature, Old Delhi kebabs, Nizamuddin biryani. Thirty days of travel, thousands of kilometres of track, and India stays unpredictable, intense and overwhelming right through to the final meal.

visits.text: Dharamsala is about 5 hours by road from Amritsar - the road gradually climbs toward the Himalayan foothills. Your destination is McLeod Ganj, 'little Lhasa,' perched at 1,770m altitude. Since 1959, this is where the Dalai Lama has lived, after fleeing Chinese-occupied Tibet. Begin with the Tsuglagkhang complex, the spiritual heart of the Tibetan community in exile. The main temple houses a 3-meter golden statue of Buddha Shakyamuni, flanked by Avalokiteshvara and Padmasambhava. The atmosphere is peaceful - monks in crimson robes, prayer wheels spinning, whispers of mantras.

Right next door, the Kalachakra Temple contains frescoes depicting the 722 deities of the mandala, painted over three years by three master painters under the Dalai Lama's personal direction. Photography is forbidden, but the detail level of the mandalas is stunning.

Visit the Tibet Museum at the complex entrance. On two floors, it traces Tibet's history as an independent nation, the Chinese occupation since 1949, and exile. Testimonies from political prisoners and archival photos are deeply moving. Allow at least 45 minutes.

Walk down to Namgyal Monastery, the Dalai Lama's personal monastery, founded in 1564. If you're there around 1:30pm (except Sundays), you can watch the monks' debates in the courtyard - a fascinating spectacle where arguments are punctuated with theatrical hand-clapping. The monks are usually delighted to chat with visitors.

activities.text: Walk to Bhagsu Waterfall, about 2km from McLeod Ganj. The trail crosses Bhagsu village and climbs toward the waterfall through rocks. Bring walking shoes. At the top, several cafés with valley views serve momos (Tibetan dumplings) and butter tea.

Wander McLeod Ganj's lanes - Tibetan shops sell singing bowls, thangkas (fabric paintings), woolen shawls, and turquoise jewelry. Cafés serve authentic Tibetan cuisine: thukpa (noodle soup), steamed or fried momos, and the famous yak butter tea. A world apart from the Indian plains.
Take a thangka painting or Tibetan cooking class - several centers in McLeod Ganj offer half-day workshops. It's a concrete way to touch Tibetan culture beyond surface tourism.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: Arrival at Ernakulam Junction after the night train. Take the local ferry (15 minutes) to Fort Kochi - it's the best way to arrive, with the silhouette of Chinese fishing nets appearing from the boat. These enormous cantilever nets, called 'Cheena Vala' in Malayalam, were allegedly introduced by Chinese merchants in the 14th century, possibly by explorer Zheng He's fleet. You won't find them anywhere else in India.

Step through the door of St. Francis Church, the oldest European church in India. Built in wood by Portuguese Franciscans in 1503, then rebuilt in stone in 1516, it housed Vasco da Gama's burial after his death in Kochi in 1524 - his remains were later repatriated to Lisbon in 1539. Portuguese tombstones are on the north wall, Dutch ones on the south. The church is open from 9am to 5pm (except Sunday morning).

Continue to Mattancherry for the Dutch Palace, built by the Portuguese around 1555 then renovated by the Dutch in 1663. Don't be fooled by the modest exterior: inside, the 17th-century murals illustrating the Ramayana are among Kerala's finest. Right next door, Jew Town and the Paradesi Synagogue (1568) - one of the Commonwealth's oldest active synagogues - are worth the detour for their hand-painted floor tiles imported from China.

activities.text: Fort Kochi is best explored on foot - the lanes are lined with Portuguese, Dutch and British colonial houses, interspersed with contemporary art galleries and cafes in former spice warehouses. If the Kochi Biennale (every 2 years, December to March) is on, art installations take over historic buildings - Aspinwall House, the old harbor warehouse, is the main site.

In the evening, attend a Kathakali performance (Kerala's dance-theater with spectacular makeup) at one of Fort Kochi's cultural centers - shows generally start around 6:30pm with a makeup demonstration 1 hour before. Otherwise, return to the Chinese nets at sunset: fishermen operate the wooden counterweights, and the low light on the nets creates Kerala's most photographed cliché.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Hampi, Goa and the Nilgiri Hills

places.name: Goa

visits.text: Old Goa, 10 km from Panaji, is the historic heart of Portuguese colonisation — the 'Rome of the East' in the 16th century. The Basilica of Bom Jesus (1605), UNESCO-listed, houses the body of Saint Francis Xavier in a silver coffin. The Jesuit missionary died in 1552, but his body is said to be 'incorrupt' — it's publicly displayed every ten years (last time in 2024). The Baroque interior is restrained compared to the façades, but the gilded altarpiece is striking.

Just across the way, the Sé Cathedral is the largest church in Asia — 76 metres long, built between 1562 and 1640. It has only one tower (the second collapsed in 1776 and was never rebuilt). Inside, the Chapel of the Blessed Sacrament is lavished in gold. Allow 30 minutes for both churches together.

In the afternoon, head south to the beaches — Palolem is a crescent-shaped cove fringed with coconut palms, bookended by rocky headlands at each end. The water is calm, ideal for swimming. It's Goa's most photogenic beach, but also the busiest in peak season. If you're after more peace and quiet, continue to Agonda, 5 km further north — same scenery, fewer people.

Panaji (Panjim), Goa's capital, has a hidden Latin Quarter: Fontainhas. This tangle of narrow streets lined with houses painted yellow, blue and ochre is the last vestige of the old Portuguese district. The wrought-iron balconies, azulejos (decorative tiles) and corner chapels could easily teleport you to Lisbon. Allow an hour to wander with camera in hand.

Chapora Fort, made famous by Indian cinema, offers a 360° panorama over the coastline — Vagator beach below and the mouth of the Chapora River. The red laterite walls stand out sharply against the sky. Only ruins remain (the fort dates to 1717), but it's the viewpoint, not the architecture, that makes this place. Climb up in the late afternoon for the best light.

activities.text: Taste Goan cuisine — a unique blend of Indian and Portuguese influences. Vindaloo (pork marinated in vinegar and spices) and xacuti (chicken with coconut and spices) are the signature dishes. Pair them with feni, the local spirit distilled from cashew nuts or coconut palm sap.
Sunset on the beach — it's a ritual in Goa. Beach shacks serve grilled fish with your feet in the sand as the sun plunges into the Arabian Sea. A simple, perfect moment.

If you're here on a Friday, don't miss the Mapusa Market — the most authentic in Goa. Spices, chouriço sausages, tropical fruits, dried fish and local handicrafts. It's loud, colourful and thoroughly local.

In the evening, prepare for the Goa → Kochi leg: the Netravati Express (16345) departs Madgaon around 10:40 PM and arrives at Ernakulam Junction the next day around 12:20 PM — roughly 14 hours overnight. This is the famous Konkan Railway, one of the most beautiful train lines in the world: the track hugs the west coast, crossing 2,000 bridges and 92 tunnels between mountains and sea. Book an AC2 sleeper for a comfortable night. The Mangala Lakshadweep Express is another daily option. Reserve 4–5 days ahead on IRCTC.

visits.text: Hampi has been a UNESCO site since 1986—the ruins of the former Vijayanagara Empire capital (14th-16th centuries) sprawl across 26 km² among enormous ochre granite boulders. Begin with Virupaksha Temple, the only one still active, dedicated to Shiva. Its 50-meter gopuram tower dominates the main bazaar. Inside, in a dark chamber, you'll witness an astonishing phenomenon: the inverted image of the gopuram projects naturally onto the wall through a camera obscura effect. Arrive early morning to beat the heat—it can be crushing after 11am.

Continue to Vittala Temple, 2.5 km northeast. This is Hampi's jewel: its stone chariot, drawn by carved elephants, appears on 50-rupee notes. The 56 musical pillars in the main hall produce different notes when struck—a 16th-century engineering feat. Take a tuk-tuk or rent a bicycle to connect the two temples; walking in full sun is exhausting.

activities.text: Late in the day, climb Matanga Hill for sunset. The ascent takes about 20 minutes via a rocky trail—wear good shoes and bring water. From the summit, the 360° view over the ruins, rice paddies, Tungabhadra River, and granite boulders is one of Karnataka's most beautiful. Descend before dark—the path isn't lit.

To explore the ruins further afield, rent a bicycle for the day and tour the royal enclosure: the Lotus Mahal (a pavilion in Indo-Islamic style), the elephant stables (eleven aligned domes), and the Queen's Bath. Allow 2 to 3 hours to cover this area by bike at a leisurely pace.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: Haridwar is only 25km from Rishikesh but the vibe is totally different - here, no hippies or trendy cafés. It's one of Hinduism's seven holiest cities, where the Ganges officially enters the plains. The city's heart is Har Ki Pauri, the most sacred ghat, where a footprint of Vishnu is said to be carved in stone. This is where millions of pilgrims come for ritual bathing. Every 12 years, Haridwar hosts the Kumbh Mela, the world's largest human gathering - over 100 million people in 2021.

Take the cable car (or walk up, 45 min) to Mansa Devi Temple, perched on a Shivalik ridge 300 meters above the city. The panoramic view sweeps over Haridwar, the Ganges, and the Gangetic plains stretching to the horizon. The temple is dedicated to goddess Mansa, who grants wishes - you'll see red threads tied by devotees.

On the opposite hill, Chandi Devi Temple (also cable-car accessible) allegedly dates from the 8th century and was founded by philosopher Adi Shankaracharya. The walk up (3km) crosses a forest with valley views - pleasant in the morning when it's cool.

activities.text: Attend Ganga Aarti at Har Ki Pauri at sunset - it's older and more 'authentic' than Rishikesh's, less packaged for tourists. Thousands of devotees crowd the ghats, temple bells ring, incense smoke mingles with river mist. Place a diya (floating lamp) on the Ganges and watch it drift in the current.

Wander through Bara Bazaar, Haridwar's main market. You'll find vessels for carrying Ganges water home (a sacred souvenir for Hindus), rudraksha (prayer beads), deity statues, and traditional sweets. The atmosphere is frenetic and colorful.

Sample Haridwar's strict vegetarian cuisine - the entire city is vegetarian (alcohol and meat are banned). Dhabas (eateries) around Har Ki Pauri serve complete thalis, kachori, and thick lassi. Haridwar's chole bhature is famous across North India.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: See the Hawa Mahal (Palace of Winds) early when the sun strikes the facade—that's the magic hour. This 15-meter pink sandstone wall, built in 1799 by Maharaja Sawai Pratap Singh, contains 953 honeycomb windows. Architect Lal Chand Ustad designed it in the shape of Krishna's crown. The jharokhas allowed royal women to observe street processions without being seen. Surprising detail: the building has no foundation. Entry is from the rear, not the facade—search carefully.

Jaipur has been a trading city since its founding in 1727 by Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh II—one of India's first grid-plan cities. Johari Bazaar for gemstones, Tripolia Bazaar for textiles and lac bangles, Bapu Bazaar for souvenirs. Stop for roadside chai—often offered by shopkeepers hoping you'll linger.

The City Palace of Jaipur is still partially inhabited by the royal family. The Mubarak Mahal (Palace of Welcome), built in the late 19th century, houses a royal textile museum. Don't miss the two giant silver urns in the Diwan-i-Khas—standing 1.60 m tall, they're the world's largest solid silver objects (Guinness record). They carried Ganges water on royal trips to England.

Right next to the City Palace, Jantar Mantar is an astronomical observatory built in 1734 by Sawai Jai Singh II. Its 19 monumental stone instruments are a UNESCO site since 2010. The most imposing: the Samrat Yantra, the world's largest sundial—27 meters tall, accurate to 2 seconds. Hire a guide on-site, or the instruments remain incomprehensible.

If you have energy late in the day, head to Galtaji (Monkey Temple) 10 km east. Nestled in a rocky gorge, this 18th-century temple complex is overrun by macaques. Natural pools fed by springs are sacred. The main temple dedicated to Hanuman is decorated with murals. Guard your belongings from the monkeys—they're quick and opportunistic.

Start with Nahargarh Fort at dawn—the view over Jaipur from the ramparts is the city's finest. Built in 1734 by Sawai Jai Singh II then expanded in 1868, the fort dominates the Aravalli ridge. Inside lies Madhavendra Bhawan, with its symmetrical suites built for the maharaja's nine queens. If you're athletic, cycle up from the city—the road is steep but spectacular.

Descending, stop at Jal Mahal (Water Palace) for the photo. This 18th-century palace seems to float in the middle of Man Sagar Lake—in reality, four of its five floors are submerged. You can't enter, but the view from the shore, especially with the Aravalli backdrop, is one of Rajasthan's classic postcards.

The royal cenotaphs of Gaitore, nestled in a valley between Nahargarh and the city, are often ignored by tourists. These are the tombs of Jaipur's maharajas—the most elaborate, in carved white marble, are those of Sawai Jai Singh II and Sawai Pratap Singh. The place is peaceful and photogenic, especially in the morning.

Amber Fort is the most spectacular monument in the Jaipur region. Built from 1592 by Raja Man Singh I on a hill overlooking Lake Maota, it blends Rajput and Mughal architecture. The Sheesh Mahal (Mirror Palace) inside is dazzling—thousands of convex mirror fragments line walls and ceilings. The Diwan-i-Aam courtyard and the Ganesh Pol (with its colored tile mosaic) are unmissable. The fort is part of the six Rajasthani forts inscribed as UNESCO in 2013. Allow at least 2 hours.

Just below Amber Fort, don't miss Panna Meena ka Kund—a 16th-century geometric stepwell whose zigzagging staircases create a hypnotic pattern. Highly photogenic, and far less crowded than Abhaneri's Chand Baori.

If Indian textiles interest you, head to the Anokhi Museum in Amber, housed in a restored haveli. It traces the history of block printing (hand-stamping on fabric), an ancestral Rajasthani technique. Artisan demonstrations are often offered.

activities.text: Take a guided bicycle tour of Jaipur at dawn, before the heat and traffic. Several operators offer 2-3 hour circuits through the old city, with stops at the flower and vegetable markets, and breakfast with a local family.
Late in the day, settle on the ramparts of Amber for sunset. The golden light on the Aravalli hills and Lake Maota below composes a sumptuous tableau. Bring water—there are no vendors at the top.

visits.text: Jaisalmer Fort is unique in the world: it's one of the last fortresses still inhabited, home to around 3,000 people living within its golden sandstone walls. Founded in 1156 by Rawal Jaisal, it rises 76 metres above the Thar Desert on Trikuta Hill. The golden stone has earned it the nickname 'Sonar Quila' (Golden Fort) — at sunset, the ramparts seem to melt into the sand. Inside, visit the 12th-century Jain temples, particularly the Chandraprabhu Temple with its mind-boggling stone carvings — every square centimetre of stone is intricately chiselled.

Head down to the lower town to explore the havelis — grand old mansions of fabulously wealthy merchants, carved from the same golden sandstone as the fort. Start with Patwon Ki Haveli, the largest — five adjoining buildings constructed between 1805 and 1862 by a family that traded in brocade and opium. Every balcony, every window is a lacework of stone. Then move on to Nathmal Ki Haveli, built by two brothers who each worked on one side — spot the subtle differences between the two halves; it's a fun game.

Gadisar Lake, a 10-minute walk from the fort, is an artificial reservoir dug in 1367 by Maharawal Gadsi Singh. The entrance is through a carved gateway. Early in the morning, the fort's reflection in the still water is stunning. You can rent a pedal boat or simply sit on the ghats.

activities.text: Take an excursion into the Thar Desert to the Sam Sand Dunes, 42 km from Jaisalmer. Operators offer late-afternoon outings to catch the sunset over the dunes — by camel or by jeep. The sight of golden dunes bathed in raking light is breathtaking. If you spend the night under a tent, the Thar's starry sky, free from light pollution, is one of the most spectacular in India. You can also return the same evening if you'd rather sleep in town.

days_plan.itin_title: India Grand Tour: From the Taj Mahal to the Backwaters
Intensive Rajasthan Road Trip: Forts, Tigers & Taj Mahal

places.name: Jodhpur

visits.text: Mehrangarh dominates Jodhpur from a 125-meter rocky spur—it's one of India's most massive forts. Built from 1459 by Rao Jodha, it houses a remarkable museum with an excellent audio guide available in French. The collections of palanquins, armor and royal cradles are exceptional. Pass through Loha Pol gate—you'll see the handprints of queens who committed sati (immolation on their husband's funeral pyre). Allow 2 to 3 hours for the complete visit.

From Mehrangarh's ramparts, you'll understand why Jodhpur is the 'Blue City'—the indigo blue of the Brahmin quarter's houses stretches endlessly below. Descend into the lanes to lose yourself in this labyrinth of bright blue facades, carved doors and nonchalant cows. The best viewpoints are from the guesthouse terraces in the Navchokiya quarter.

Jaswant Thada, a 5-minute walk from Mehrangarh, is a white marble cenotaph built in 1899 in memory of Maharaja Jaswant Singh II. The marble is so thin it's translucent in sunlight. The garden and small artificial lake surrounding it make this a haven of peace after the fort's intensity.

Below the Clock Tower, Sardar Market is Jodhpur's beating heart—spices (Rajasthani red chili, turmeric), lac bangles, tie-dye textiles and miniatures. If you're seeking the city's best lassi, follow the locals—the most popular stalls are always the most crowded.

Push on to Toorji Ka Jhalra, an 18th-century stepwell recently restored, hidden among the old city houses. Less monumental than Bundi's, but the turquoise water and geometric stairs make it an unexpected photo spot.

Mandore Gardens, 9 km north of Jodhpur, shelter the cenotaphs of the Marwar rulers in a shaded park. The dewals (funerary temples) in red sandstone are carved with Hindu figures. The place is frequented by local families in the morning—a good glimpse of daily life away from tourist circuits. Watch for langurs, which are numerous and bold.

Rao Jodha Desert Rock Park, at the foot of Mehrangarh, is a 72-hectare ecological reserve created to restore desert vegetation. Marked trails traverse a landscape of volcanic rocks and endemic plants—euphorbias, commiphoras, acacias. If you enjoy walking, it's the perfect complement to the fort visit, with different views of the ramparts.

Umaid Bhawan Palace, part luxury hotel and part royal family residence, merits at least an outside glance. Built between 1929 and 1943, it's one of the last great palaces constructed in India—347 rooms, in sandstone and marble. The interior museum displays Art Deco collections and palace models.

activities.text: Operators offer zipline descents above Mehrangarh's ramparts and lakes—6 lines spanning 1.2 km with plunging views over the blue city and fort. Thrills guaranteed. Book the day before.

Set out on an excursion to the Bishnoi villages, 30 km from Jodhpur. The Bishnoi are a community renowned for their nature protection since the 15th century—they live in harmony with local wildlife (antelopes, gazelles, demoiselle cranes). Several operators offer half-day circuits that also include visits to potter and weaver artisans.
