visits.text: Enjoy Varkala in the morning—descend the steps to Papanasam Beach (the "beach that washes away sins") for a morning swim. The water is warm year-round and the waves are moderate. Freshwater springs gush from the cliff directly onto the beach—a rare phenomenon. Head back up the cliff stairway, stopping at the little perched cafés along the way.

Take a ferry from Kanyakumari to reach Vivekananda Rock, 500 meters offshore. It was on this rock that the Hindu monk Swami Vivekananda meditated in 1892 before delivering his famous speech at the Parliament of Religions in Chicago. The memorial, built in 1970, blends architectural styles from across India. Right next to it, the statue of Thiruvalluvar—a 2nd-century Tamil poet—rises 40 meters above the ocean. Ferries depart every 30 minutes and the crossing takes 10 minutes. Avoid weekends and public holidays—the queue can last 2 hours.

activities.text: Kanyakumari is the southernmost point of India, where the Indian Ocean, Arabian Sea, and Bay of Bengal meet. Sunset from the Sunset View Point is the highlight: you'll see the sun plunge into the sea at the exact spot where three waters converge. Arrive 30 minutes early to secure a good spot—all of Kanyakumari gathers here.

End your circuit with a stroll along the Kanyakumari seafront. The seashell stands, chai stalls, and sea breeze offer a peaceful conclusion to your journey. The nearest airport is Trivandrum (Thiruvananthapuram), 90 km to the north—allow 2 hours' drive to reach it.

days_plan.itin_title: Tamil Nadu and Kerala Road Trip: Temples and Backwaters

visits.text: The Chettinad region, 90 km south of Trichy, is one of Tamil Nadu's best-kept secrets. In the 75 villages around Karaikudi, more than 10,000 palatial mansions were built by the Nattukottai Chettiars, a merchant community that made fortunes in maritime trade with Southeast Asia during the 19th and 20th centuries. The mansions blend Italian marble columns, Burmese teak, European stained glass, and handmade Athangudi tiles—a combination unique in the world. Many are closed, but a few like the VVR Palace welcome visitors for a modest fee.

The Kanadukathan Palace, 10 km from Karaikudi, is the most spectacular of the Chettinad mansions. Built in 1912 by Dr. Annamalai Chettiyar (founder of Indian Bank and Annamalai University), it contains a hundred rooms adorned with Spanish marble columns and crystal chandeliers. Visiting requires advance permission—ask your accommodation to arrange this. A few of these mansions have been converted into heritage hotels—sleeping in one is an experience in itself.

activities.text: Chettinad cuisine is renowned throughout India for its powerful spices—black pepper, star anise, fennel, and dried chili. If your accommodation offers it, don't miss a traditional meal served on a banana leaf. The Chettinad Chicken you may know from restaurants will be on a completely different level here—warning, it's seriously spicy.

Explore the villages on foot—every street corner reveals a new palace, a colorful temple, or a sacred tank. The Ayyanar temples, village guardians, with their giant terracotta equestrian statues, are typical of this region. Drive slowly between hamlets and let yourself be surprised by the scale of these mansions in the middle of the Tamil countryside.

days_plan.itin_title: Tamil Nadu and Kerala Road Trip: Temples and Backwaters

visits.text: Khajuraho is about 4 hours from Orchha. UNESCO-listed since 1986, it's one of India's most extraordinary sites. Of the 85 temples built by the Chandela dynasty between 950 and 1050 AD, 25 have survived - and their sculptures are stunningly refined. Start with the Western Group, the most spectacular. Kandariya Mahadeva temple is the largest and most elaborate: its walls are covered with nearly 900 sculptures depicting deities, celestial nymphs (apsaras), and the famous entwined couples (mithuna). Contrary to belief, erotic scenes represent only about 10% of sculptures - the rest shows daily life, war, music, and dance.

Lakshmana temple, dedicated to Vishnu, is one of the best preserved. Built under King Yashovarman around 950, it rests on a high platform adorned with a continuous frieze of sculptures - elephants, horses, battle scenes, and court life. Take time to walk completely around the temple to appreciate the sculpture progression, from the earthly world at the base toward the divine at the summit.

Don't neglect the Eastern Group, 20 minutes' walk away. The Jain temples of Parsvanath and Adinath are less famous but equally finely carved, with floral motifs and apsaras of remarkable grace. The contrast between Hindu and Jain temples side by side testifies to the Chandela era's religious tolerance.

activities.text: Attend the sound and light show within the Western Group enclosure - it recounts Chandela history and temple construction. The show lasts about 50 minutes and takes place after sunset. The dramatic nighttime lighting of the temples alone is worth the trip.
Explore Khajuraho's small Archaeological Museum, which displays sculptures recovered from destroyed temple sites. Some pieces match the quality of those still in place on the temples.

Walk through Khajuraho village late afternoon - it remains surprisingly rural and quiet despite tourism. Local markets sell stone and wood carvings that imitate (with varying success) the temple style.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

places.name: Kochi

visits.text: You land at Cochin International Airport in Nedumbassery, roughly 40 km from Fort Kochi. Plan on a solid hour's drive to reach the historic quarter—the route cuts through paddy fields and coconut-lined canals that set the tone for the entire journey. Check into your accommodation and take time to acclimatize to Kerala's humid warmth.

Late afternoon, head to Fort Kochi Beach promenade to witness the Chinese fishing nets—massive cantilevered structures of teak and bamboo mounted on granite counterweights, in use since the 14th century and introduced, according to tradition, by merchants from Kublai Khan's court. The spectacle peaks at sunset, when fishermen work the nets by sheer muscle power. If hunger strikes, vendors right beside the nets will grill the fresh fish you pick from their catch.

Start at St. Francis Church, built of timber in 1503 by Portuguese Franciscan friars—it's the oldest European church in India. The current stone building dates to 1516. Vasco da Gama was buried here in 1524 during his third voyage to India; his remains weren't repatriated to Lisbon until 14 years later, but you'll still see his original tombstone inside. The church passed from Portuguese hands to the Dutch in 1663, then to the British in 1795—each occupant left its mark. Free entry, open 9am–5pm except during Sunday services.

A few minutes' walk brings you to Santa Cruz Cathedral Basilica, originally erected by the Portuguese in 1505 and rebuilt in its present neo-Gothic form in 1902. The interior will surprise you—pastel ceiling frescoes and a richly carved altar offer a burst of color that contrasts sharply with the whitewashed exterior. Stained-glass windows filter soft light into the nave, lending it a contemplative atmosphere even at midday.

Catch an auto-rickshaw or ferry to Mattancherry to visit Paradesi Synagogue, built in 1568 by Sephardic Jews who fled Spain and Portugal. The floor is paved with hundreds of hand-painted porcelain tiles imported from Canton in the 18th century—each one unique. 19th-century Belgian crystal chandeliers hang above a brass pulpit. The synagogue also preserves copper plates engraved in ancient Malayalam 1,600 years ago, detailing privileges granted to the Jewish community by the Cochin raja. Note: closed on Fridays, Saturdays, and Jewish holidays. Enter barefoot.

Exiting the synagogue, lose yourself in the alleys of Jew Town and Mattancherry spice market. The air is thick with cardamom, pepper, turmeric, and cloves—the same spices that drew Phoenicians, Romans, Arabs, and Europeans to this coast for millennia. Antique shops overflow with colonial furniture, vintage porcelain, and old coins. Bargain, but keep in mind that prices here are already more reasonable than Delhi or Mumbai.

The drive from Varkala to Kochi (184 km, 3.5 hours) takes you back north. If you leave early, you'll have time to visit Mattancherry Palace (also called the Dutch Palace), built by the Portuguese in 1555 as a gift to the Cochin raja, then renovated by the Dutch in 1663—hence its nickname. Don't miss the 17th-century murals illustrating scenes from the Ramayana and Mahabharata—they rank among Kerala's finest wall paintings. The palace also houses royal portraits, palanquins, and ceremonial garments.

If your flight is in the evening, use the remaining time to explore Jew Town's antique shops—vintage ceramics, textiles, and brass objects. Or enjoy one last Keralan meal at a Fort Kochi restaurant: a full thali with rice boat (rice shaped like a boat), fried fish, mango pickle, and payasam for dessert. Cochin International Airport is about 1 hour 15 minutes from Fort Kochi—allow a comfortable margin.

activities.text: Wander through Fort Kochi's lanes as dusk falls. Portuguese, Dutch, and British colonial facades succeed one another within a few hundred meters—an architectural palimpsest found nowhere else in India. You'll stumble upon contemporary art galleries installed in former spice warehouses, cafés tucked into Dutch houses, and street art murals. The atmosphere is serene, a world apart from the chaos of India's megacities.

In the evening, attend a Kathakali dance performance. This four-century-old theatrical form from Kerala blends dance, mime, and music. Artists spend over an hour applying makeup—green faces for heroes, red for villains—using natural pigments layered on thickly. Arrive 30 minutes before the show to watch the makeup application, often open to the public. Count on around 1.5 hours for the full experience.

Before leaving Kerala, if time allows, swing by Broadway Market in Ernakulam (the mainland part of Kochi). This covered market, far less touristy than Mattancherry, is where locals buy their spices, fabrics, and kitchen utensils. You'll find black pepper, green cardamom, and turmeric powder at unbeatable prices—perfect for useful souvenirs.

visits.text: Train from Gaya (closest station to Bodhgaya) to Kolkata (about 7 hours - take the Rajdhani or Duronto Express). Kolkata, formerly Calcutta, was the British Raj capital until 1911. It's a city of violent contrasts: colonial splendor and misery, boiling intellectual culture and urban chaos. Start with Victoria Memorial, a white marble palace built between 1906 and 1921 honoring Queen Victoria. Its architecture blends Italian Renaissance, Mughal, and Venetian styles. The interior museum traces Calcutta's history under the British - paintings, weapons, manuscripts, and a reconstruction of the 'Black Hole of Calcutta.'

Walk to Howrah Bridge (Rabindra Setu), the world's busiest steel bridge - about 100,000 vehicles and countless pedestrians cross it daily. Built in 1943 without any bolts or rivets (entirely riveted), it spans the Hooghly over 705 meters. The best time to cross on foot is early morning, when flower merchants carry their marigold garlands to Mallick Ghat market just below.

Visit the Indian Museum, India's oldest and largest museum, founded in 1814. Its collections are sprawling: dinosaur fossils, Gandhara Buddhist sculptures, Mughal miniatures, Egyptian mummies, meteorites, and an entire gallery dedicated to Indian textiles. Allow 90 minutes minimum - the building is immense.

Tour St Paul's Cathedral, eastern India's largest Gothic building, built in 1847. The stained glass and wooden framework recall English churches, but the tropical climate imposed modifications - airy verandas and large windows. The contrast with Hindu temples is striking.

Second day in Kolkata, devoted to authentic Kolkata. Start with Mallick Ghat flower market, under Howrah Bridge - arrive before 7am to see mountains of orange marigolds, roses, and jasmine unloaded from boats. The colors and energy are incredible. It's Asia's largest flower market, with over 2,000 vendors.

Push on to Kumartuli, the potter artisans' quarter where Durga statues are made for the annual Durga Puja festival - Kolkata's biggest celebration. Hundreds of workshops open to the street mold straw and clay statues reaching up to 5 meters tall. Even outside festival season, watching artisans at work is worth the trip.

Visit Kalighat Temple, one of Hinduism's 51 Shakti Peethas, dedicated to goddess Kali - indeed, Kali gave Calcutta its name. The current temple dates from 1809, but the site has been venerated for centuries. The atmosphere is intense: lines of devotees, flower and incense offerings, priests in trance. Non-Hindus are generally accepted in the outer courtyard.

Head to College Street, the world's largest concentration of bookstores after Buenos Aires. Sidewalks disappear under piles of books - university textbooks, Bengali novels, Tagore poetry, and all sorts of rarities. Coffee House café, an institution since 1942, was Bengali intellectuals' meeting place - Satyajit Ray, Amartya Sen, and Subhas Chandra Bose all hung out here.

activities.text: In the evening, dine at one of Park Street's restaurants, Kolkata's gastronomic artery. Bengali cuisine is considered one of India's most refined - fish (hilsa is king), mustard, five-spice blend (panch phoron), and rasgulla (fresh cheese balls in syrup) for dessert. Kolkata is also the birthplace of puchka (Bengali panipuri) - street vendors serve them everywhere.
Ride a Kolkata tram - it's India's last operating tram network, in service since 1902. The rattling cars cross congested streets at snail's pace - it's not efficient transport, it's an experience.

Visit Mother Teresa's House (Missionaries of Charity), where the saint lived and worked from 1953 until her death in 1997. Her tomb lies in the ground floor chapel. A small museum traces her life and work among Kolkata's poorest. Entry is free and the place is imbued with moving simplicity.

Take a boat ride on the Hooghly (Ganges branch) at sunset. From the water, you'll see the illuminated Howrah Bridge pass by, Kolkata's ghats, and old colonial mansions lining the river. Several operators offer one-hour cruises from Prinsep ghats.

End the evening with a Bengali classical dance or music performance - Kolkata is India's cultural capital, birthplace of Satyajit Ray's cinema and Ravi Shankar's music. Nandan and Rabindra Sadan halls regularly program events.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas
Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: Leave Alleppey southward for Kollam (70 km, 2 hours), one of the world's oldest trading ports. Marco Polo described it in the 13th century under the name "Coilum," and Ibn Battuta in the 14th century counted it among the five greatest ports he'd seen in 24 years of travel. Phoenicians, Romans, Chinese, and Arabs traded spices, silk, and porcelain here for millennia. Today, the town is the global cashew capital—you'll catch the roasting aroma in certain neighborhoods.

Visit Ashtamudi Lake, Kerala's second-largest body of water, a Ramsar-designated wetland. Its name means "eight arms" in Malayalam, referring to the eight channels branching through the mangroves. It's the southern gateway to the backwaters, far quieter than Alleppey. If you haven't had your fill of canals, a short cruise on Ashtamudi will offer wilder scenery, with coconut palms leaning over the water and sleepy fishing villages.

Detour to Munroe Island (Munroethuruthu), an archipelago of eight small islands at the confluence of Ashtamudi and the Kallada River. You reach it by boat. Life here has remained virtually unchanged—copra drying, toddy (palm wine) production, shrimp fishing in the canals. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a boat tour with a local guide.

activities.text: Late in the day, climb Thangassery Lighthouse, a relic of Portuguese and then Dutch presence on this coast. From the top, the view sweeps across the Arabian Sea and the red roofs of the old colonial quarter. Thangassery was the first European trading post established in Kollam in 1502. Catch the sunset from the adjacent beach.

days_plan.itin_title: Kerala Backwaters, Tea Hills & Coast Circuit

visits.text: Leave Mumbai via the Mumbai-Pune Expressway (115 km, 2.5 hours) heading to Lonavala, a hill station perched in the Western Ghats. The road snakes through tunnels and verdant valleys—during monsoon, waterfalls gush everywhere, but in the dry season the landscape is more arid.

Your first stop: Karla Caves, which house India's largest rock-cut Buddhist chaitya (prayer hall). Built between the 2nd century BC and 2nd century AD, this hall measures 45 meters long and 14 meters high—an underground cathedral carved from basalt. What strikes you first are the teak wood ribs on the ceiling: most are original, still in place after more than 2,000 years. The 37 octagonal pillars are topped with sculptures of couples riding elephants—a liveliness and naturalism that makes you forget their age. Count on 350 steps to climb from the base of the hill.

Eight kilometers from Karla, Bhaja Caves are a complex of 22 Buddhist caves from the 2nd century BC, contemporary with Karla but far less crowded. You'll find stupas, remarkable narrative sculptures, and an atmosphere of contemplative solitude that Karla has lost to tourism. Access requires about 200 steps on a rocky path—the views across the valley from the top are worth the effort.

activities.text: Late in the day, enjoy Lonavala—the town itself isn't exceptional, but the fresh Ghats air and views from viewpoints like Tiger's Leap or Rajmachi Point are welcome after Mumbai's heat. Taste chikki, the local specialty—a crunchy treat made from jaggery (unrefined cane sugar) and peanuts, sold everywhere in town.

days_plan.itin_title: Mumbai & Maharashtra Circuit: Sacred Caves and the Konkan Coast

visits.text: The Meenakshi Amman Temple is arguably the most spectacular temple in all of South India — and the one monument that justifies the detour to Madurai on its own. Its 14 gopurams (gateway towers) soar up to 52 metres high and are covered in thousands of brightly painted sculptures — gods, goddesses, demons, mythical creatures. The current temple dates from the 17th century under the Nayak dynasty, but a sanctuary has stood here for at least 2,000 years. Inside, 33,000 sculptures adorn the corridors. The sacred Golden Lotus Tank (Potramarai Kulam) sits at the heart of the complex. Visit early in the morning (opens at 5 AM) or in the evening — the ceremony of putting Shiva and Meenakshi to bed around 9 PM is an intense experience. Note: no shoes or shorts, and non-Hindus do not have access to the inner sanctum.

After the temple, pop over to the Thirumalai Nayakkar Mahal, a 17th-century palace built by King Thirumalai Nayak in 1636. Only a quarter of the original building survives (the king's grandson dismantled the rest to build a new palace in Trichy), but step into the main hall: its massive 12-metre-high columns give you a sense of past grandeur. A sound and light show takes place in the evening.

activities.text: The Madurai flower market, right beside the Meenakshi Temple, is a festival of colours and fragrances — mountains of jasmine (Madurai's flower), roses and marigolds pile up in baskets. Tamil women wear jasmine garlands in their hair, and the flowers serve as temple offerings. Come early morning when the deliveries arrive.

The Gandhi Memorial Museum, housed in the former Rani Mangammal Palace, traces the Mahatma's life. The most moving exhibit is the bloodstained dhoti Gandhi was wearing when he was assassinated on January 30, 1948. The museum is well curated and free.

Get ready for your Madurai → Rameswaram journey: express trains connect the two cities in 3–4 hours for 170 km. The highlight of the ride is the Pamban Bridge — India's second-longest rail bridge (2.06 km), built in 1914, which crosses the sea on stilts to link Rameswaram Island to the mainland. The train literally runs above the ocean. Grab a window seat.

days_plan.itin_title: India Grand Tour: From the Taj Mahal to the Backwaters
Tamil Nadu and Kerala Road Trip: Temples and Backwaters

visits.text: Mahabalipuram (Mamallapuram) has been a UNESCO site since 1984—it's one of India's oldest rock art ensembles. Begin with the Shore Temple, built in the 8th century by Pallava king Narasimhavarman II. This granite temple facing the sea is one of South India's oldest structural stone temples. Two towers dedicated to Shiva and a Vishnu sanctuary stand facing the Bay of Bengal waves. Arrive at opening time (6am) to enjoy the golden morning light and tranquility—tour buses arrive after 9am.

500 meters south, the Five Rathas (Pancha Rathas) are five monolithic temples each carved from a single granite block in the 7th century. Each bears the name of one of the Pandava brothers from the Mahabharata and illustrates a different architectural style—from simple to complex. It's like an architectural textbook in stone. In front of the largest ratha, a life-size elephant was carved from the same rock.

Don't miss the bas-relief of Arjuna's Penance (or Descent of the Ganges), a sculptural fresco 27 meters long and 9 meters high carved into a rock face. Hundreds of figures—gods, humans, animals, nagas—converge toward a central fissure representing the Ganges descending from heaven. It's one of the world's largest bas-reliefs. Just beside it, Krishna's Butter Ball is an enormous spherical boulder of 250 tons balanced on a slope—no one has ever managed to move it, not even the British with their elephants.

activities.text: Late in the day, head to Mahabalipuram beach. Less touristy than Chennai's beaches, it's lined with fishing catamarans and small restaurants serving grilled fish with your feet in the sand. Walk south toward the new fishing harbor to watch boats returning at dusk.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: The Dharamsala to Manali road is long (about 8 hours) but spectacular - it winds through the Kangra and Kullu valleys, between apple orchards, turquoise rivers, and snowy peaks. Plan stops for the views. Once in Manali, settle in and start with Hadimba Devi Temple, nestled in a cedar forest 2km from town. Built in 1553 by Maharaja Bahadur Singh, it's a four-tiered wooden and stone pagoda temple, dedicated to Hadimba, a demoness from the Mahabharata who became a goddess after marrying warrior Bhima. The architecture is unique in India - the wooden doors are carved with detailed mythological figures.

Climb up to Old Manali, 2km upstream from the modern town. This stone and wood village has kept its Himalayan charm despite the influx of trendy cafés and guesthouses. Traditional wooden and slate houses cling to the slope, and lanes open onto clear views of the peaks. This is where the hippie community settled in the 1970s - the vibe remains laid-back.

Detour to Vashisht, a thermal village 3km from Manali. Natural hot springs feed public pools where locals and travelers bathe side by side. The Vashisht temple, dedicated to the Vedic sage of the same name, is a fine example of Himachali wooden architecture with detailed carvings on the lintels.

Second day in Manali, dedicated to the heights. Head to Solang Valley (14km, 30 min), a natural amphitheater surrounded by snowy peaks. Depending on the season, you'll find paragliding, zorbing, and quad rides (summer), or skiing and sledding (winter). The cable car takes you up to 3,000m altitude with plunging views over the Kullu valley. Arrive early - crowds show up after 10am.

If the pass is open (May to November), push on to Rohtang Pass at 3,980m altitude - the name means 'pile of corpses' in Persian, recalling travelers who died attempting the crossing. The 51km road from Manali crosses landscapes that change dramatically: deodar forests, then alpine meadows, then bare rock and snow. At the summit, the view embraces the Pir Panjal glaciers on one side and Lahaul valley on the other. Bring warm clothes even in summer. A permit is mandatory - request it online the day before.

Alternative if Rohtang is closed: take the Atal Tunnel, the world's longest high-altitude road tunnel (9.02km), inaugurated in 2020. It connects Kullu valley to Lahaul valley in 30 minutes instead of 4 to 6 hours via the pass. On the other side, Sissu village and its waterfall offer a first glimpse of desert Lahaul - a striking contrast with Manali's greenery.

activities.text: Walk along the Beas River, which flows through the Kullu valley. In season (May to July, September to October), operators offer whitewater rafting between Pirdi and Jhiri - a 14km stretch with class II to III rapids, perfect for a first experience.

Taste Himachali specialties: siddu (steamed bread stuffed with nuts and spices), madra (chickpeas in yogurt sauce), and fresh trout caught from the Beas, grilled or curried. Old Manali restaurants have the best menus.

Late in the day, climb to Manu Temple, dedicated to sage Manu, considered in Hindu mythology as humanity's creator - Manali takes its name from him. The temple is modest but the path offers clear valley views.

Try tandem paragliding from Solang Valley or Marhi - Himalayan thermals carry you above the valley for 15 to 20 minutes. The sensation of soaring facing the 6,000-meter massif is unforgettable. Operators offer the flight with GoPro video included.

Stop at Vashisht hot springs on the way back if you didn't visit them yesterday. After a day at altitude, a bath in naturally hot sulfurous water is the best remedy for fatigue. Locals come at dawn - late afternoon you'll have more space.

places.name: Mumbai

visits.text: You land in Mumbai, and the sensory assault begins the moment you step out of the airport: humid heat, honking horns, the scent of jasmine mingling with exhaust fumes. Grab a taxi or Uber to Colaba or Fort, the historic heart of South Mumbai. Settle in and brace yourself—this city of 20 million doesn't do anything by halves.

Start at the Gateway of India, the monumental yellow basalt arch that rises 26 meters above the waterfront. Completed in 1924 by Scottish architect George Wittet, it was built to commemorate King George V's 1911 visit—except the king only saw a cardboard model, as construction hadn't yet begun. History's irony: the last official act of the British Empire in India played out here on February 28, 1948, when the 1st Battalion of the Somerset Light Infantry marched through this arch for the final time, with a 21-gun salute. Directly opposite stands the Taj Mahal Palace Hotel, opened December 16, 1903 by industrialist Jamsetji Tata—India's first luxury hotel, which actually predated the Gateway by two decades.

Walk north to Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus (CST), formerly Victoria Terminus—the most spectacular train station you'll ever see. A UNESCO site, it was designed by architect Frederick William Stevens in Victorian neo-Gothic style blended with Indian and Islamic elements, completed in 1888 after ten years of construction. Over 3 million passengers pass through daily—making it one of the world's busiest rail hubs. Look up: turrets, stained glass, gargoyles, an octagonal dome crowned by an allegorical statue of Progress holding a torch. You might recognize it—this is where Slumdog Millionaire ends.

A stone's throw from CST, push on to Crawford Market (officially Mahatma Jyotiba Phule Mandai), a Victorian market completed in 1869. The white marble bas-reliefs at the entrance, depicting scenes of rural Indian life, are the work of John Lockwood Kipling—father of Rudyard Kipling, author of The Jungle Book. Inside, it's an assault of colors and smells: pyramids of Alphonso mangoes in season, mountains of turmeric, Kashmiri saffron, loose spices. Arrive before 10 AM for the raw energy of the market in full swing.

Catch the first ferry from the Gateway of India to Elephanta Caves (about 1 hour crossing). The island houses rock-cut temples carved into basalt between the 5th and 7th centuries, UNESCO-listed since 1987. The centerpiece is the Trimurti Sadashiva—a three-faced Shiva over 6 meters tall, representing the Creator, Protector, and Destroyer. It's one of India's largest rock-cut sculptures, and the finesse of the expressions—serenity in the central face, feminine gentleness on the right, ferocity of Bhairava on the left—stops you in your tracks. The Portuguese, who used the sculptures for target practice in the 17th century, caused considerable damage, but what remains is magnificent. NOTE: Closed Mondays. Allow 3 to 4 hours for the complete visit.

Back in Mumbai, stop at Dhobi Ghat, the world's largest open-air laundry, in operation for over 120 years. From the pedestrian bridge at Mahalaxmi station, you'll see hundreds of washermen beating clothes in concrete troughs stretching as far as the eye can see—a hypnotic spectacle and a stark reminder that in this city of billionaires, manual labor still reigns.

Next, visit Mani Bhavan, the house where Gandhi lived from 1917 to 1934 during his Mumbai stays. It was from here that he launched the non-cooperation and civil disobedience movements that changed India's history. The house-museum, preserved in its original state, contains a library of 20,000 volumes, Gandhi's handwritten letters, and period photographs. The atmosphere is sober and contemplative—a striking contrast with the chaos of the street just below.

If you're an early riser and brave, wake at 5 AM for Sassoon Docks—Mumbai's oldest and largest wholesale fish market, founded in 1875. The Koli, the fishermen who are Mumbai's original inhabitants (the name "Mumbai" comes from Mumbadevi, their goddess), unload the day's catch in a frenzy of shouts, crushed ice, and silvery fish. On the warehouse walls, the St+art Foundation has painted giant murals that transform this raw workplace into an open-air art gallery.

Start with Haji Ali Dargah, a 15th-century mosque-mausoleum perched on a rocky islet in the Arabian Sea, connected to the mainland by a 500-meter causeway that vanishes underwater at high tide. The shrine is dedicated to merchant Haji Ali Shah Bukhari, who reputedly abandoned everything after a pilgrimage to Mecca. The atmosphere is powerful: the sea wind, waves licking the causeway, Sufi chants echoing under the white marble dome. Check tide times before going—if the causeway is submerged, you won't be able to access it.

Head next to the CSMVS (Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sangrahalaya, formerly Prince of Wales Museum), an Indo-Saracenic building from 1922 designed by George Wittet—the same architect as the Gateway of India. The collections span 5,000 years of Indian art: Mughal miniatures of extraordinary finesse, Gandhara sculptures (the Greco-Buddhist art of northwest India), weapons, textiles, and archaeological pieces from the Indus Valley Civilization. The palm garden surrounding the museum is a peaceful haven amid the city's din. Allow a good 2 hours.

In the afternoon, venture to Kanheri Caves, in Sanjay Gandhi National Park—104 km² of tropical forest right in Mumbai, an improbable green lung in this megacity. The 109 Buddhist caves, carved from the 1st century BC to the 10th century AD, form one of India's largest rock-cut monastic complexes. Cave 3, called the Great Chaitya, is a 25-meter-long prayer hall with carved pillars and a central stupa. The journey from South Mumbai takes about 1.5 hours—plan a half-day for the round trip and visit.

The return from Nashik to Mumbai (170 km, 3.5 hours) brings you back to the megacity for your final day. Start with a place most tourists miss: Banganga Tank, a 12th-century sacred pool nestled among the skyscrapers of Malabar Hill, one of Mumbai's most expensive neighborhoods. According to legend, the god Rama, thirsty during his journey to Sri Lanka to rescue Sita, shot an arrow that made a tributary of the Ganges spring forth at this spot. The pool, surrounded by small ruined temples and ancient houses, exudes a village atmosphere frozen in time—200 meters from the glass and steel towers. It's one of Mumbai's best-kept secrets.

Head to Kala Ghoda, Mumbai's arts district. This small perimeter of pedestrian streets and colonial buildings concentrates art galleries, independent bookstores, design boutiques, and cafés. The annual Kala Ghoda Festival in February transforms the district into an open-air cultural celebration. Even outside festival season, it's the ideal place to feel the city's creative pulse, far from the clichés of poverty and chaos.

End with Chor Bazaar—literally the "Thieves' Market"—in the Mutton Street neighborhood. This labyrinth of alleyways is Mumbai's largest flea market: antiques, colonial furniture, Bollywood film props, old clocks, gramophones, Art Deco door handles salvaged from demolished buildings. Legend says you can find a stolen object here a few days after losing it. Bargain hard—opening prices are inflated for tourists, but the atmosphere is good-natured.

activities.text: End the day with a sunset stroll along Marine Drive—3 km of seafront curve facing the Arabian Sea, nicknamed the "Queen's Necklace" when the streetlights illuminate at night. The Art Deco buildings from the 1930s-40s lining the promenade form one of the world's largest concentrations of Art Deco architecture, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. What makes this district unique is that these buildings were designed by Indian architects—the first major architectural movement led by Indians during the colonial period. Sit on the concrete parapet with the Mumbaikars, buy bhel puri from a street vendor, and watch the sun plunge into the Arabian Sea.

In the evening, explore the Colaba Causeway district—a commercial artery lined with shops, cafés, and restaurants. Dine at one of the city's historic Iranian restaurants, like Leopold Café (open since 1871) or Café Mondegar with its walls painted by caricaturist Mario Miranda. Mumbai's street food is legendary—try a vada pav (Mumbai's "burger"), pav bhaji, or masala dosa from the stalls at Chowpatty Beach.

Returning from Kanheri, if time permits, make a detour through Bandra—Mumbai's "Brooklyn." The Bandra-Worli Sea Link, the 5.6 km cable-stayed bridge spanning the bay, offers spectacular views of Mumbai's skyline. The neighborhood teems with trendy cafés, galleries, and restaurants. The Bandstand Promenade at sunset is a classic.

Before heading to the airport, treat yourself to one last Mumbaikar meal. If you want refined dining, Fort district restaurants offer high-end contemporary Indian cuisine. If you prefer authentic, one last vada pav from a street stall will be your Mumbai madeleine. Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport is 45 minutes to 1.5 hours from South Mumbai depending on traffic—and in Mumbai, traffic is always an unpredictable variable. Allow plenty of time.

visits.text: The drive from Alleppey to Munnar takes 4 to 5 hours, but it's magnificent—you pass from flat paddy fields to the first hills of the Western Ghats, with waterfalls along the way (stop at Cheeyappara if you have time). Munnar sits at 1,600 m altitude, and tea plantations stretch as far as the eye can see across the hills. The first plants were installed here by Scottish planters in the late 19th century. Wander through the rows of tea bushes—the Kannan Devan estate alone covers 24,000 hectares. For a real immersion, ask to observe the morning picking (7am-9am), when women in saris move through the fields with their large baskets.

Step into the Munnar Tea Museum (Kannan Devan Tea Museum), 3.5 km from town center. Inaugurated in 2005 by the Tata Group, it traces the history of the plantations from the British pioneers. You'll see a manual tea roller from 1905, a 1920s Pelton wheel that powered the factories, and a locomotive wheel from the railway that ran between Munnar and Top Station. The museum offers two demonstration sessions per day (10am and 2pm) and a paid tasting of different teas. Closed Mondays. Allow 45 minutes to an hour.

Second day in Munnar: head to Mattupetty Dam, 13 km from town center. This artificial lake surrounded by tea-covered hills and shola forests is one of the region's most beautiful spots. You can take a motorboat tour on the lake, or simply walk along the shore to enjoy the calm. On the way, stop at Echo Point—shout toward the hills and your voice bounces back clearly. It's touristy, but kids love it.

Continue to Kundala Lake, 20 km from Munnar. This high-altitude lake is surrounded by tea plantations and forests. Pedal boats and shikaras (small traditional boats from Kashmir) are available for a leisurely ride. The road there winds through postcard-perfect landscapes—keep your camera ready.

activities.text: Wake before dawn to watch sunrise from Munnar's hills. The most spectacular viewpoints are Pothamedu (5 km from town center) and Top Station (32 km away, Munnar's highest point at 1,880 m). When the mist lifts from the green undulations of the plantations, the spectacle is breathtaking. Bring a sweater—at this altitude, mornings are cool, often around 10°C.

If you have energy to spare, hike in Eravikulam National Park, 15 km from Munnar. This 97 km² park in the Western Ghats is home to the endangered Nilgiri tahr, a wild goat that's surprisingly approachable here. The main trail runs about 2 km round-trip across grassy slopes. Every twelve years, these hills blanket themselves in blue neelakurinji flowers—the next bloom is expected in 2030.

Between sites, make a stop at Chinnakanal Waterfalls (also called Power House Falls), 18 km from Munnar. Water tumbles from 2,000 m altitude through dense vegetation—a particularly impressive sight after the monsoon. The path to get closer is short but slippery, so wear good shoes.

End the day with a jeep tour of a tea plantation. The Lockhart estate, accessible only by 4x4, offers an experience away from the crowds: you drive between rows of tea bushes, watch the manufacturing process in the mini-factory, and taste freshly brewed tea. Book in advance through your accommodation—spaces are limited.

visits.text: Munroe Island is an archipelago of eight islets at the confluence of Ashtamudi Lake and the Kallada River, 25 km from Kollam. Here, no crowded houseboats like in Alleppey—you glide in a dugout canoe, maneuvered with a bamboo pole, through channels so narrow that palm fronds brush your shoulders. The island takes its name from Colonel John Munro, a British administrator of Travancore in the 19th century. Book a morning paddle of 2 to 3 hours: you'll pass through natural mangrove arches, drift past houses on stilts, and cross paths with fishermen casting their nets. This is the peaceful, authentic alternative to the touristy backwaters.

After the canoe ride, explore the village by bicycle—most homestays lend bikes free of charge. The 5 km loop around the island is flat and shaded by coconut palms. You'll traverse coconut plantations, paddy fields, and coir-making workshops. Stop at Edachal bridge to watch fishermen at work, and if you come across a coir workshop, ask for a demonstration—artisans have been hand-braiding the fiber for generations.

activities.text: Late in the day, join a Kerala cooking class at a local home. Your host will show you how to prepare a traditional fish curry with coconut milk, appam (fermented rice pancakes), and aviyal, a mixed vegetable dish typical of Kerala. You'll eat everything served on a banana leaf, sitting on the floor in local style. It's also a chance to taste toddy, a mildly alcoholic drink tapped from coconut palm sap—the local specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: The Maharaja's Palace of Mysore is one of India's most visited monuments after the Taj Mahal. Built between 1897 and 1912 by British architect Henry Irwin, it blends Indo-Saracenic, Gothic, and Hindu styles. The durbar (audience) halls are covered in gold leaf, stained glass, carved columns, and solid silver doors. The golden throne, made of fig wood plated with 280 kg of gold, is brought out only during the Dasara festival in October. Allow 1.5 hours for the visit. Important: you must remove your shoes, and cameras are prohibited inside.

After the palace, dive into Devaraja Market, a ten-minute walk away. This covered bazaar, one of Mysore's oldest, overflows with pyramids of flowers (jasmine, roses, marigold), spices, tropical fruits, and silks. Don't leave without trying the crispy dosas from the stalls—Mysore masala dosas, stuffed with spiced potatoes and green chutney, are a local specialty. If you have a sweet tooth, Mysore pak (a ghee and chickpea flour cake) is a classic.

activities.text: On Sundays and public holidays, as well as during Dasara, the palace lights up with 97,000 bulbs at 7pm. The spectacle lasts about an hour and transforms the building into a golden jewel against the night. Even on weekdays, stroll through the illuminated gardens. Arrive 30 minutes early to secure a good spot—crowds can be dense.

If you have time in the afternoon, climb Chamundi Hill (1,065 m), 13 km from town center. You can drive up or climb the 1,000 steps. Along the way, you'll pass the monolithic Nandi, 5 meters high and carved from a single block of black granite. At the summit, Chamundeshwari Temple offers a panorama over the entire city and surrounding plains.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: The road from Aurangabad to Nashik (225 km, 4.5 hours) crosses the Deccan plateau before descending toward the verdant hills of the Nashik valley. Nashik is one of Hinduism's holiest cities—it hosts the Kumbh Mela every 12 years, the world's largest human gathering (tens of millions of pilgrims). The Godavari River, which Hindus consider the "Ganges of the South," flows through the city.

Explore the sacred ghats along the Godavari—Ramkund is the most important, the place where according to tradition Rama bathed during his exile. Pilgrims bathe here to wash away their sins, and this is where funerary ash immersion rites take place. The atmosphere is intense: temple bells, chants, marigold flowers floating on the water, priests in white dhotis.

If time permits, push on to Trimbakeshwar, 30 km from Nashik—one of the 12 Jyotirlinga (Shiva's most sacred temples in India). The current black stone temple, built in the 18th century by Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao, is surrounded by green hills. It's also the source of the Godavari, which rises on Brahmagiri hill just above. The sanctuary interior is reserved for Hindus, but the exterior architecture and atmosphere are worth the visit.

For archaeology enthusiasts, Pandavleni Caves (or Trirashmi) are a complex of 24 Buddhist caves carved between the 3rd century BC and 2nd century AD—among Maharashtra's oldest. Less spectacular than Ajanta, they offer a glimpse of primitive Buddhist monastic architecture in a peaceful setting.

activities.text: Surprise: Nashik is India's "wine capital." Its altitude (around 600 m), dry climate, and volcanic soils create conditions close to California's Napa Valley. Several wineries are open to visitors—Sula Vineyards, the pioneer, offers tastings with views over the lake and vines. York Winery and Grover Zampa are other good options. End the day with a glass of local Chenin Blanc watching the sunset over the hills—an unexpected contrast with the morning's millennia-old temples.

days_plan.itin_title: Mumbai & Maharashtra Circuit: Sacred Caves and the Konkan Coast

visits.text: Long road from Agra (about 7 hours) but Orchha is worth every kilometer. This city founded in the 16th century by Rajput chief Rudra Pratap Singh, on the banks of the Betwa, is one of central India's hidden gems. A UNESCO World Heritage candidate, it has kept a charm that Jaipur or Udaipur lost long ago. Start with Jehangir Mahal, built by King Bir Singh Deo to welcome Mughal emperor Jahangir. Its circular towers, fluted domes, and jali balconies blend Mughal and Rajput styles with rare elegance. Climb to the roof - the view over the river and cenotaphs is sumptuous.

Right next door, the older Raja Mahal preserves wall frescoes of surprising beauty for a building of this era. Peacocks, elephants, and geometric motifs dance across the Darbar Hall walls. Narrow passages and spiral staircases add to the feeling of exploration - you often have rooms to yourself.

Walk down to the cenotaphs (chhatris) along the Betwa - 14 funerary monuments honoring Bundela kings, built on high stone platforms with panchayatan-style domes. Their reflection in the river water at sunset is one of India's most photogenic images. Climb to the top of Bir Singh Deo's cenotaph for a panorama over all of Orchha. Bonus: a colony of long-billed vultures (critically endangered species) nests in the structures.

Visit Ram Raja Temple, India's only temple where Rama is worshipped as a king rather than a god. Uniformed guards render him military honors every day. The neighboring Chaturbhuj temple offers magnificent views from its accessible roof, and Laxmi Narayan temple (1662) preserves detailed wall frescoes on its interior walls.

activities.text: Attend the Jehangir Mahal sound and light show in the evening, which traces Bundela dynasty history. The palaces illuminated at night create a magical atmosphere.
Rent a bicycle and tour Orchha at your own pace - the town is compact enough to explore everything by pedaling. Paths along the Betwa cross bucolic landscapes with palaces in the background.

places.name: Palolem

visits.text: Arrival in Palolem, southern Goa - and the contrast with Hampi is striking. It's a 1.6 km crescent bay of white sand lined with coconut palms, with shallow water ideal for swimming. A former fishing village turned traveler spot, Palolem maintains a laid-back atmosphere compared to north Goa. Settle into one of the beach huts and enjoy this first day with your toes in the sand.

Late afternoon, walk south along the beach to Patnem Beach - an even quieter cove, separated from Palolem by a rocky headland (10 minutes' walk). Patnem is smaller, with fewer shacks but an almost meditative atmosphere. The water is clear and the sandy bottom slopes gently.

Excursion to Cabo de Rama Fort, perched on a headland overlooking the Arabian Sea. Its name comes from the Ramayana - Rama and Sita are said to have stayed here during their exile. The fort existed well before the Portuguese, who took it in 1763 from the Raja of Soonda and fortified it with a chapel dedicated to Saint Anthony. It served as a prison from 1935 to 1955. Today in ruins, there remain rusted cannons on the bastions and the small white church. Entry is free - push to the western edge for a vertiginous view over the cliffs and ocean.

Coming down from the fort, detour to Cola Beach - an isolated beach accessible by dirt track. Cola's special feature is its freshwater lagoon just behind the beach, separated from the sea by a sand spit. The contrast between the lagoon's blue water and the Arabian Sea waves is striking. Few people, no noisy shacks - this is secret Goa.

Take a local bus or taxi to Margao (Madgaon), the capital of south Goa, 40 km away. The town retains a strong Portuguese imprint: colonial houses in pastel facades line the old quarter. Step through the door of the Church of the Holy Spirit, built in 1675 - its white baroque facade dominates the central square, and the interior contains a remarkable gilded altarpiece.

Margao Municipal Market is a festival for the senses: mountains of spices (pepper, cinnamon, cardamom), fresh fish spread on ice, unknown tropical fruits. It's the busiest market in south Goa - go in the morning when the stalls are full. Count on a good hour to tour it without rushing.

activities.text: Take a kayak from the northern end of Palolem to reach Butterfly Beach - a cove accessible only by sea or jungle trail. You can see butterflies between October and March, and the beach is rarely crowded. The kayak crossing takes 15-20 minutes - it's also a chance to spot dolphins early in the morning.

Sunset at Palolem faces west: the sun descends between the bay's two rocky headlands. This is when the shacks light candles and fishermen pull up their colorful boats. Stay for dinner - the grilled catch of the day with kokum curry (local sour fruit) is the local specialty.

The south Goa coastal road between Palolem and Cabo de Rama (about 20 km) passes through fishing villages, coconut groves and viewpoints over coves. By scooter, it's a treat - stop whenever a view tempts you. Fill up with petrol before leaving, there are no stations between the two.

If you have time on the return, make a detour to Agonda Beach - a long beach less crowded than Palolem, lined with palms and a few wooden cabanas. The vibe is very relaxed, with possibilities for yoga and ayurveda in centers set up along the beach.

If timing allows, head down to Colva Beach - a huge white sand beach very popular with Goans on weekends. The contrast with the southern coves is clear: here it's livelier, with shacks lined up and a family atmosphere. Count on 15 minutes by taxi from Margao.

Prepare departure to Kochi: from Madgaon (Margao station), the Mangala Lakshadweep Express or Netravati Express connect to Ernakulam Junction (Kochi) in 10-14 hours depending on the train. Overnight journeys are ideal - you save a day. Book your berth in advance via IRCTC.

places.name: Puducherry

visits.text: Train from Thanjavur → Puducherry via Trichy (~4–5h with a connection). Puducherry (formerly Pondicherry) is India's old French outpost — handed over by France in 1954. The French Quarter (Ville Blanche) is a grid of quiet streets lined with mustard-yellow colonial houses, bougainvillea spilling from balconies and street signs in French. Rue Suffren, Rue Dumas, Rue Romain Rolland — you could almost forget you're in India. Except the sacred cows haven't read the street names.

Walk down to Promenade Beach, which runs 1.5 km along the Bay of Bengal — an esplanade flanked by colonial buildings, a Gandhi statue and the French war memorial. Cars are banned in the morning and evening, making for a pleasant stroll. At the northern end, spot the old lighthouse and Bharathi Park.

The Sri Aurobindo Ashram, in the heart of the French Quarter, is the spiritual centre around which Puducherry has built its identity since the 1920s. Sri Aurobindo, philosopher and independence activist, settled here in 1910 to escape the British police. The ashram now occupies several buildings — the samadhi (tomb) of Sri Aurobindo and the Mother, covered in flowers, sits at the centre of a silent courtyard. Admission is free and open to all, but silence is expected.

Paradise Beach (Playa Paraiso), reachable only by boat from Chunnambar Boat House (15-minute ride through the backwaters), is a strip of sand between the river and the sea. Wilder than Promenade Beach, it's fringed with casuarina trees and nearly deserted on weekdays.

activities.text: Take an excursion to Auroville, 12 km from Puducherry — a 'universal city' founded in 1968 by Mirra Alfassa ('The Mother'), the spiritual companion of Sri Aurobindo. The Matrimandir, a golden sphere 29 metres in diameter at the community's centre, is the iconic image of the place. Visiting the Matrimandir requires advance booking — without it, you'll only see the outer gardens. Around 3,000 people of 60 nationalities live in Auroville.

Dinner in the Tamil Quarter (Ville Noire) for a striking contrast with the French side — dosa and idli in the morning, chettinad at lunch, and French crêpes in the evening. Puducherry is the only city in India where you can switch between a café au lait and a filter coffee without crossing a border.

Rent a bicycle and explore the French Quarter and its surroundings — it's the best way to get around Puducherry. The streets are flat and distances short. Swing by Goubert Market (near the beach) for tropical fruits and flowers.

Get ready for your Puducherry → Chennai journey: trains connect the two cities in about 3.5 hours for 150 km. The Villupuram–Chennai Express is a daily option. You can also take an air-conditioned Volvo bus (3 hours direct). Book on IRCTC.

visits.text: The drive from Lonavala to Pune is short (65 km, 1.5 hours). Pune, Maharashtra's second city, was the former capital of the Maratha Empire—the one that stood up to both the Mughals and the British. Start with Shaniwar Wada, the fortified palace of the Peshwas (Maratha prime ministers), built in 1732. A mysterious fire in 1828 destroyed almost everything—only the imposing perimeter walls and massive gates remain. The Delhi Gate, the main entrance, bristles with 72 steel spikes designed to prevent war elephant charges. The contrast between the grandeur of the ruins and the emptiness inside is striking—imagine this palace at its peak, when the Peshwas ruled an empire stretching from southern to northern India.

Next, visit Aga Khan Palace, an elegant 1892 building surrounded by gardens, whose history is inseparable from India's independence struggle. The British imprisoned Gandhi, his wife Kasturba, and his secretary Mahadev Desai here from 1942 to 1944, during the "Quit India" movement. Kasturba died here on February 22, 1944—a memorial and samadhi (cremation site) mark the spot in the gardens. The museum inside traces Gandhi's life through photographs, letters, and personal objects. The atmosphere is emotionally charged.

For Indian craft enthusiasts, the Raja Dinkar Kelkar Museum houses a staggering collection of 20,000 art objects assembled by one man over his lifetime: musical instruments, ornate locks, oil lamps, weapons, carved doors, palanquins. It's a cabinet of Indian curiosities that makes your head spin.

activities.text: Pune is a dynamic student city—spend the evening exploring the Koregaon Park neighborhood, with its cafés, restaurants, and bars. The culinary scene is diverse: from traditional Maharashtrian restaurants to trendy cafés. Try misal pav—Pune's signature dish, a spicy sprouted lentil curry served with soft bread rolls, topped with farsan (crunchy garnish).

visits.text: Pushkar Lake is one of Hinduism's few sacred lakes—according to legend, it was created when Brahma dropped a lotus petal to earth. Its 52 ghats (sacred steps) descend to the water where pilgrims bathe and make flower offerings. Remove your shoes when approaching the ghats and be discreet—this is a place of prayer, not a photo backdrop. Beware fake priests who insist on tying a red thread to your wrist then demand a 'donation.'

Pushkar's Brahma Temple is one of the very rare temples worldwide dedicated to Brahma, the creator god. The entrance is marked by a marble gate and the floor is paved with silver tiles. The temple is small but the fervor of devotees is intense. Opens at 6am—go early to avoid the queues.

Climb to the Savitri Temple, perched on the hill overlooking Pushkar. The walk up takes 30-40 minutes (a cable car also exists). From the summit, the view over the lake, temples and surrounding dunes is Pushkar's finest, especially at sunrise or sunset.

Wander through the lanes of Pushkar bazaar—a surprising mix of hippie souvenir shops, incense stalls and street food stands. Pushkar is a vegetarian town (no alcohol or meat in the town center restaurants) due to its sacred character. Try malpua, a local sweet fried pancake, the town's specialty.

activities.text: If your trip coincides with Holi (March), Pushkar is one of India's premier locations for the festival of colors. Celebrations around the lake are organized and highly festive—bring clothes you can sacrifice and protect your cameras with a waterproof bag.

visits.text: The Ramanathaswamy Temple is one of the four Char Dham, the most sacred pilgrimage sites in Hinduism. According to legend, this is where Rama prayed to Shiva to purify himself after killing the demon king Ravana. The temple possesses the longest corridors of any Hindu temple in the world—197 meters bordered by 1,212 sculpted granite columns. Inside, 22 sacred wells (theerthams) represent the 22 arrows from Rama's quiver. Pilgrims are sprinkled with water from each well—each is said to cure a different ailment. Allow a minimum of 2 hours and leave your cameras outside.

Before reaching Rameswaram, you'll have crossed the Pamban Bridge—a 2.3 km structure built in 1914 that connects the island to the mainland over the Palk Strait. It's the only road link to the continent and the view of the turquoise ocean on both sides is spectacular. Stop at the bridge viewpoint for a few photos before entering the island.

Push on to Dhanushkodi, 20 km east of Rameswaram—the extreme eastern tip of the Indian subcontinent. This ghost village was destroyed by a cyclone in 1964 that killed more than 1,800 people. Only the ruins of a church and railway station remain, engulfed by sand. At the end of the track, you'll see the sandbanks of Adam's Bridge (Rama Setu), that alignment of shoals extending toward Sri Lanka 30 km away. The place is otherworldly and striking—go in the morning to avoid the heat.

activities.text: The ritual bathing at Agni Theertham, the sacred beach facing the temple, is a spectacle in itself. Pilgrims immerse themselves in the ocean before entering the temple. Even if you don't practice the rite, the beach at sunrise, with the fishermen and devotees, offers a powerful moment in your journey.

Wander through Rameswaram's colorful alleys in late afternoon. The flower stalls, seashell vendors, and chai shops give the atmosphere of a pilgrimage town very different from tourist beach resorts. The sunset on the island's western shoreline, facing the mainland, is peaceful.

visits.text: Rishikesh - the 'world capital of yoga' - sits where the Ganges leaves the Himalayan foothills for the plains. The atmosphere is radically different from Delhi's chaos: here cows, sadhus in orange, and backpackers in yoga pants coexist on the ghats. Start by crossing Ram Jhula, the 137-meter suspension bridge built in 1986, linking ashrams on both banks. The view of the turquoise Ganges, framed by forested hills, sets the tone.

Visit the Beatles Ashram (officially Chaurasi Kutia), Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's former ashram where John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, and Ringo Starr stayed in 1968 to study Transcendental Meditation. Abandoned for decades, the site has reopened to visitors and the ruined buildings are covered with colorful graffiti and murals honoring the Fab Four. They composed much of the White Album here. The place is strangely peaceful despite its fame.

Walk down to Swarg Ashram, the pedestrian quarter on the Ganges' left bank. No cars here - only lanes lined with ashrams, temples, and small vegetarian restaurants. Bharat Mandir, Rishikesh's oldest temple, is dedicated to Vishnu and allegedly dates from the 12th century.

Late afternoon, join Triveni Ghat or Parmarth Niketan for Ganga Aarti - the evening ceremony where dozens of priests wave large oil lamps toward the river while mantras resound. Hundreds of small floating lamps (diyas) are placed on the water. The atmosphere is intense and deeply moving - arrive 30 minutes early to find a good spot on the steps.

activities.text: Go rafting on the Ganges - Rishikesh is India's number one spot for this activity. Several routes from 9 to 36km are available, with class II to IV rapids. The classic Shivpuri to Rishikesh stretch (16km, 3-4h) passes the 'Roller Coaster' and 'Golf Course' rapids. Book the day before with local operators.

Take a one or two-hour yoga class at one of countless ashrams. Even if you've never practiced, this is the dream place to start - beginner classes are everywhere. The International Yoga Festival happens every March at Parmarth Niketan.

visits.text: Ranthambore Fort perches 213 meters above the forest, at the heart of the national park—the only major Indian fort located entirely within a tiger reserve. Founded around 944 by Chauhan king Sapaldaksha, a UNESCO site since 2013, it changed hands about ten times over the centuries. Climb the 273 steps to the summit to discover the Trinetra Ganesh temple—the world's only one where Ganesh is depicted with three eyes. Langurs populate the ruins, and occasionally a tiger prowls below. The view over the park from the ramparts is spectacular.

Outside safari hours, stroll around Padam Talao and the Raj Bagh ruins at the park entrance. The lakes harbor crocodiles basking in the sun, sambar deer and langur hordes. The area is rich in birdlife—purple herons, crested serpent eagles, kingfishers. Bring binoculars.

activities.text: Dawn jeep safari in Ranthambore National Park. The 392 km² park shelters around 70 Bengal tigers—a rare trait: they're diurnal, which increases the odds of spotting them in broad daylight. The 6-seater jeep safari lasts 3 hours. Book online several weeks ahead—zones 2, 3 and 4 offer the best chances. Bring warm layers for the winter morning, and keep your eyes on the langurs: their alarm call often betrays a nearby cat.

visits.text: Before leaving the region, detour to Auroville, 12 km north of Puducherry. This "universal city" founded in 1968 by "the Mother" brings together roughly 3,500 residents from 60 nationalities. The Matrimandir—a golden sphere 29 meters in diameter covered with gold discs—is the most photographed structure in Tamil Nadu after the temples. You won't be able to enter the sphere without advance reservation, but the Visitors' Centre offers good panoramic views from the exterior viewpoints. Allow 1.5 hours on site.

Get back on the road toward Thanjavur (175 km, roughly 4 hours). The Cauvery plain you'll cross is Tamil Nadu's rice bowl—paddy fields stretching to the horizon, dotted with village temples and their colorful gopurams. Arrive in late afternoon for an initial visit to the Brihadisvara Temple, a UNESCO World Heritage site since 1987. Built in just seven years (1003–1010) by the Chola emperor Rajaraja I, it's the largest all-granite temple in India. The main tower (vimana) rises 66 meters—the capstone weighs 80 tons and is said to have been hoisted via a 6 km ramp. Visit in late afternoon when the low-angle light brings out the granite's texture.

activities.text: Before leaving Auroville, browse the craft shops at the Visitors' Centre—pottery, incense, organic cotton clothing. It's the most interesting place to buy local handicrafts in the Puducherry region.

If you reach Thanjavur before closing time, soak up the evening ritual atmosphere at Brihadisvara: priests light oil lamps and chanting echoes beneath the granite vaults. The monolithic Nandi (Shiva's sacred bull), carved from a single 6-meter block, stands guard at the entrance—devotees touch its horns to receive blessings.

visits.text: The road from Rameswaram to Thekkady (roughly 300 km, 7–8 hours) crosses the Western Ghats and the landscape changes dramatically: you pass from the dry Tamil Nadu plains to Kerala's green hills covered with tea, coffee, and spice plantations. The final winding stretch through the mountains is spectacular. Arrive in late afternoon in Kumily, the gateway village to the Periyar Reserve.

Visit a spice garden in the Kumily area to understand how black pepper, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and vanilla grow. The guides will have you smell, taste, and touch the plants—you'll never look at your spice rack the same way again. Kerala cardamom is considered the finest in the world. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours for the visit.

activities.text: In the evening, don't miss a Kathakali performance, Kerala's traditional dance-theater. The dancers, made up over 2 hours before the performance, interpret episodes from the Mahabharata and Ramayana with codified facial expressions and spectacular costumes. Operators offer performances in Kumily—arrive 30 minutes before the start to watch the makeup application, which is almost as fascinating as the show itself.

visits.text: The Sri Ranganathaswamy Temple at Srirangam, a district of Trichy, is the world's largest functioning Hindu temple: 63 hectares, 7 concentric enclosures, 21 gopurams, and 50 shrines. Built from the 10th century by the Cholas, it was expanded by the Pandyas, Hoysalas, and Nayaks over the centuries. The Rajagopuram (royal tower), completed in 1987, rises 73 meters—the tallest temple tower in Asia. Allow at least 2 hours to traverse the seven enclosures to the inner sanctum where Ranganatha rests, Vishnu reclining on the serpent Adishesha.

From the areas on the rooftops of the outer galleries accessible to visitors, you'll have a striking view of the entire complex—the colorful gopurams succeed one another endlessly. A fascinating detail: the Orlov Diamond, now displayed in the Moscow Kremlin, is said to have been stolen from the idol in this temple by a French deserter in the 18th century.

Round out the day with Trichy's Rock Fort Temple, perched on an 83-meter rocky outcrop in the city center. The 437 steps carved into the rock lead to the summit where the view embraces Srirangam, the Cauvery River, and the surrounding plain. The rock itself is 3.8 billion years old—one of the oldest rock formations on Earth.

activities.text: Srirangam's ritual atmosphere peaks during evening prayer: oil lamps illuminate the corridors of sculpted columns and priests' chanting reverberates through the galleries. If you're here in December–January, the Vaikunta Ekadasi festival draws a million pilgrims over 21 days—spectacular but crowded.

days_plan.itin_title: Tamil Nadu and Kerala Road Trip: Temples and Backwaters

visits.text: Start with Padmanabhaswamy Temple in the heart of Trivandrum's East Fort. This temple dedicated to Vishnu blends Kerala and Dravidian architectural styles—its seven-tier gopuram is covered in finely chiseled sculptures. Inside, the deity's idol measures 5.5 meters long and reclines on the serpent Anantha, visible through three separate doors. In 2011, the opening of its underground vaults revealed a treasure estimated at $22 billion, making it the world's richest temple. Important note: only Hindus may enter, and there's a strict dress code—men must wear a dhoti (available for rent at the entrance) and remove their upper garment.

Just beside the temple, step into Kuthiramalika Palace. Built in the 1840s by Maharaja Swathi Thirunal, a musician and patron of the arts, this palace of teak and rosewood takes its name from the 122 carved horses adorning the consoles that support the southern roof. Of its 80 rooms, only 20 are open to the public—you'll see two royal thrones, one fashioned from 24 elephant tusks, the other from Bohemian crystal. Don't miss the musical tree, an object that produces eight different sounds when struck in different places. Closed Mondays.

Late in the day, head to Kovalam, 16 km south of Trivandrum. This former fishing village became famous in the 1930s when the Queen Regent of Travancore built Halcyon Castle here. Three crescent beaches follow one another: Lighthouse Beach is the liveliest, Hawa Beach draws Indian families, and Samudra Beach remains the quietest. Settle in at Lighthouse Beach for sunset—the golden light on the coconut palms and fishing nets is a sight not to be missed. If you have time, climb the Vizhinjam lighthouse for a panoramic view of the coast.

activities.text: In the evening, sit down for a seafood dinner facing the Arabian Sea. The beachfront restaurants in Kovalam serve grilled fish, king prawns, and crab masala accompanied by rice and coconut curry. If you're curious, try the karimeen pollichathu, a local fish cooked in a banana leaf—it's one of Kerala's signature dishes.

To recover from jet lag, book an Ayurvedic massage at one of Kovalam's wellness centers. Kerala is the birthplace of Ayurveda, and Kovalam concentrates an impressive offering of traditional centers. Expect about an hour for an abhyanga, the classic warm oil massage.

visits.text: Transit day: Jaisalmer → Udaipur is the trickiest leg of the trip. There's no direct train between the two cities. The best option is an overnight bus (roughly 10–11 hours, departing around 5–6 PM, arriving early morning) or going via Jodhpur by train (5 hours) then Jodhpur → Udaipur by car or bus (5 hours). Either way, you'll arrive in the morning. Rest at your hotel before hitting the sights in the early afternoon.

Head to the City Palace of Udaipur — the largest palace in Rajasthan, a complex stretching 244 metres long and 30 metres high, built over four centuries starting in 1559 by Udai Singh II, the city's founder. It towers over Lake Pichola from a rocky ridge. Inside, lose yourself in the maze of courtyards, balconies and chambers adorned with mirror mosaics and painted frescoes. The museum displays Rajput miniatures, weapons and royal cradles — allow at least 1.5 hours.

The Jagdish Temple, steps away from the City Palace, is a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu, built in 1651. Its 24-metre façade is covered in sculptures of dancers, elephants and horsemen. You'll recognise it by the statue of Garuda (Vishnu's eagle mount) guarding the entrance.

The Monsoon Palace (Sajjangarh) sits atop the Aravalli hills, 5 km from the centre — built in 1884 by Maharana Sajjan Singh to watch the monsoon clouds roll in, it was never completed. The winding road up passes through forested land. From the summit, the view plunges over all of Udaipur, its lakes, and hills stretching as far as the eye can see. Take an auto-rickshaw up in the morning for the best light.

Saheliyon Ki Bari (the Garden of the Maidens of Honour) is an ornamental garden built in the 18th century by Maharana Sangram Singh II for the 48 handmaidens who came as part of his queen's dowry. Fountains, lotus ponds and shaded pavilions — a peaceful haven away from the bustle of the markets.
Take a walk around Fateh Sagar Lake, Udaipur's second lake — a pleasant stroll along the banks. Boats shuttle visitors to Nehru Park, a small garden island in the middle of the lake.

activities.text: In the late afternoon, take a boat ride on Lake Pichola — the hour-long trip circles Jag Mandir and Jag Niwas (the Lake Palace, now a luxury hotel that appears to float on the water). The lake was created in 1362 and expanded by successive maharanas. The golden evening light on the white palaces is Udaipur's crowning moment.

In the evening, step inside Bagore Ki Haveli, an 18th-century haveli turned museum. A traditional Rajasthani dance performance takes place every evening at 7 PM in the courtyard — Ghoomar dances, puppet shows and acrobats. It's touristy but well done, and the candlelit courtyard atmosphere is charming.

Visit Shilpgram, a reconstructed artisan village 3 km from Fateh Sagar — 26 traditional huts representing the architectural styles of Rajasthan, Gujarat, Goa and Maharashtra. Artisans work live on-site: pottery, weaving, wood carving. It's a good place to buy genuine handicrafts at fair prices.

In the evening, organise your departure to Mumbai. The Mewar Express (train 12964) leaves Udaipur City at 6:15 PM bound for Hazrat Nizamuddin (Delhi) — but for Mumbai, an overnight bus or a domestic flight (1h15) is a better bet. Another option: a train from Udaipur → Ahmedabad (6–7h) then Ahmedabad → Mumbai (6–7h), but that's a long haul. The flight is recommended if your budget allows.

visits.text: Domestic flight Khajuraho → Varanasi (about 1h30 with stopover or direct if available). Varanasi - or Benares, or Kashi - is the world's oldest continuously inhabited city. Nothing prepares you for the sensory shock of the ghats along the Ganges: Manikarnika's funeral pyres burning 24/7, sadhus covered in ash, buffaloes bathing, fishing boats and kids playing cricket - all at once, within 50 meters. Start by descending Dashashwamedh Ghat steps, the main ghat, and let yourself be absorbed by the spectacle.

Lose yourself in the old city's lanes (galis) - a labyrinth so narrow even rickshaws can't pass. Shops overflow with Benares silk saris, jewelry, colored powders, and sweets. You'll encounter tiny temples at every corner, sacred cows, funeral processions, and playing children. It's India's most organized chaos.

Visit Kashi Vishwanath Temple (Golden Temple), one of the 12 most sacred Shiva jyotirlingas. The current temple dates from 1780, built by Queen Ahilyabai Holkar of Indore, after the previous one was destroyed by Aurangzeb. The dome is covered with 820kg of gold donated by Maharaja Ranjit Singh. Access for non-Hindus may be restricted to certain areas - inquire beforehand.

Wake before dawn for a sunrise boat ride on the Ganges - this is Varanasi's magic moment. Mist rises over the river, first golden rays touch the ghats, devotees descend the steps for ritual ablutions, pyres smoke gently. Your boatman will row you along the 84 ghats over about 5km - each has its specialty and story. Manikarnika is the main cremation ghat, where pyres never go out. Stay respectful and don't photograph cremations.

After breakfast, take an auto-rickshaw to Sarnath (10km, 30 min), the place where Buddha gave his first sermon after enlightenment - an event known as 'Setting in Motion the Wheel of Dharma.' The Dhamek Stupa, a massive 43-meter-high cylinder adorned with Gupta-era floral motifs, marks the exact spot. The Ashoka pillar that stood here bore the famous four-lion capital that became India's national emblem (visible on every rupee note).

Sarnath's archaeological museum preserves the original of Ashoka's four-lion capital (4th century BC), along with Buddhist sculptures of extraordinary grace - notably a Gupta-era Buddha considered one of the world's finest. Allow at least 45 minutes.

Back in Varanasi, walk down to Assi Ghat, the southernmost ghat, calmer than Dashashwamedh. It's the meeting point for Benares intellectuals and artists - you'll find small cafés and a relaxed vibe. The morning aarti (Subah-e-Banaras) here is more intimate than Dashashwamedh's evening one.

activities.text: In the evening, attend Ganga Aarti at Dashashwamedh Ghat. Seven young priests in saffron robes perform synchronized movements with multi-tiered oil lamps, while mantras and bells echo in the incense-laden air. The ceremony lasts 45 minutes and begins at sunset (6pm in winter, 7pm in summer). Arrive 1 hour early for a good spot on the steps, or book a boat on the Ganges to watch the spectacle from the water - it's even more impressive.
Taste silver-leaf lassi in old city shops, morning kachori sabzi, and chena dahi vada (lentil fritters with yogurt). Varanasi street food is legendary - and entirely vegetarian in the old city.

Visit a Banarasi silk weaving workshop. The silk saris woven here, with gold and silver motifs, are among India's most precious textiles. Some workshops welcome visitors and will show you the painstaking work on the loom - a sari can take from 15 days to 6 months.

Late afternoon, detour to Banaras Hindu University (BHU) campus, one of Asia's largest universities, founded in 1916. Bharat Kala Bhavan, its museum, houses a remarkable collection of Indian miniatures, sculptures, and ancient textiles.

visits.text: The drive from Kollam to Varkala is short (40 km, 1 hour)—you'll arrive mid-morning. Varkala is the antithesis of Kerala's flat beaches: here, reddish laterite cliffs 15 to 20 meters high plunge straight into the Arabian Sea. The path along the clifftop, lined with cafés, boutiques, and bougainvillea, is one of South India's loveliest coastal walks.

Descend the steps to Papanasam Beach, the main beach at the cliff's foot. Its name means "destruction of sins" in Sanskrit—Hindus believe bathing here washes away sins. The beach is monitored by lifeguards, but watch for currents—they can be strong, especially during the monsoon. Early morning, you'll see fishermen hauling nets onto the sand.

Dedicate the morning to a surf lesson on Papanasam or Black Beach. Local operators offer beginner sessions of 1.5 to 2 hours, equipment included. Waves are moderate and suited to beginners—it's one of Kerala's best learning spots. Book early morning when waves and heat are gentler.

Visit Janardhana Swami Temple, a shrine dedicated to Vishnu over 2,000 years old, perched on the cliff above Papanasam Beach. The temple is an important pilgrimage site—Keralans perform funerary rites by the sea here. The interior is reserved for Hindus, but the exterior architecture, sculptures, and atmosphere are worth the visit. A great sacred banyan shades the entrance.

In the afternoon, push north to Kappil Beach, 6 km from Varkala. This beach is bordered by a lagoon—the mouth of the Edava River meets the sea in a landscape of sand, freshwater, and coconut palms. Much quieter than Papanasam, it's ideal for a calm swim or a picnic.

activities.text: Settle on the clifftop for sunset—it's Varkala's daily ritual. Terrace cafés fill up as the sun descends toward the horizon. The vibe is laid-back, almost hippie—far from the mass tourism of Goa. If you fancy an Ayurvedic massage, reputable centers along the cliff offer traditional treatments. Stick with those affiliated to the Kerala government for quality.

Late in the day, join a Keralan cooking class. Operators in Varkala offer 2- to 3-hour workshops where you'll learn to prepare a tamarind fish curry, appam (fermented rice pancake), and payasam (coconut milk dessert). You cook and eat your own meal—a delicious way to close your penultimate evening in Kerala.
